proposals.title: A Constructed Affective Space: Mapping the Combinatorial Principles of Emotional Experience from Neurobiological Primitives
proposals.motivation: Current affective neuroscience primarily identifies neural correlates of emotional categories or dimensions, offering a descriptive catalog but not a principled, predictive system. This mirrors the pre-Periodic Table state of chemistry. A framework that explains how fundamental neurobiological components combine to construct the properties of subjective experience is needed to move beyond correlation to mechanistic explanation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a finite set of measurable neurobiological primitives—defined by spatio-temporal dynamics of large-scale networks, neuromodulator receptor density gradients, and cross-frequency coupling profiles—combine in lawful, predictable ways to instantiate the phenomenological properties (e.g., valence, arousal, granularity) of emotional episodes.
proposals.method: We will collect multimodal data (7T fMRI, M/EEG, pupillometry, and peripheral physiology) from human participants during emotion induction. Using pre-2026 methods, we will apply gradient mapping techniques (e.g., diffusion map embedding) to functional connectivity data to identify low-dimensional neural manifolds. We will then use canonical polyadic decomposition (tensor factorization) to model experiential reports (from experience sampling) as a function of the interaction between these neural gradients, physiological axes, and individual difference measures (e.g., from the Affective Style Questionnaire).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-pass, quantitative 'periodic table' for affective science, specifying a set of candidate primitives and combinatorial rules. It will shift the explanatory goal from locating emotions in the brain to explaining how emotional experiences are built from core neurobiological processes, offering a more powerful framework for predicting individual differences and clinical phenotypes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in discovering new correlates, but in applying a compositional, constructionist framework from the theory of constructed emotion to formalize the combinatorial logic linking population-level neural dynamics to experiential properties, using advanced manifold learning and factorization techniques.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Resource-Allocation Budget of Emotion: A Computational Model of Anxiety's Trade-Offs in Attention and Metabolic Expenditure
proposals.motivation: A core function of emotion is to manage the brain's finite metabolic and cognitive resources in service of allostasis. However, existing theories lack formal, quantifiable models of the specific trade-offs—such as between attentional focus and breadth—and their associated physiological costs. Grounding this in the pre-2026 literature on interoception, predictive processing, and functional brain gradients provides a crucial opportunity to move from description to computational prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that anxiety functions as an adaptive signal that reallocates metabolic (e.g., aerobic glycolysis) and cognitive resources, producing a quantifiable trade-off: it enhances performance on a focal, threat-relevant task at the direct metabolic and performance cost of degrading performance on a concurrent, peripheral perceptual task.
proposals.method: We will combine a dual-task behavioral paradigm (a central threat-detection task and a peripheral visual discrimination task) with simultaneous pupillometry, heart rate variability, and functional neuroimaging (fMRI) in human participants. We will construct a computational resource-allocation model using a hierarchical Bayesian framework to formalize the trade-off. The model will integrate behavioral performance gradients and physiological expenditure (using BOLD and pupil-linked arousal) to estimate the 'budget' parameters shifted by induced anxiety.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal, testable model of emotion as a resource-allocation mechanism, directly linking subjective experience to quantifiable gradients in brain function and peripheral physiology. It will generate specific, falsifiable principles for how the brain's metabolic budget is managed, advancing the field beyond correlational descriptions toward mechanistic, computational accounts.
proposals.novelty: While resource theories of cognition exist, applying a formal computational model to emotion's regulatory function—and directly testing it by quantifying both the performance benefit and the physiological/perceptual cost within a single framework—is a novel synthesis of Dr. Barrett's work on interoceptive gradients and brain function.

proposals.title: Mapping the Mind's Palette: A Youth-Centric Computational Platform for Simulating and Scaffolding Emotional Concept Construction
proposals.motivation: Adolescence is a critical period for developing emotional granularity and regulation, yet existing tools often frame emotion as fixed or diagnostically categorical. From the perspective of the Theory of Constructed Emotion, emotional experiences are built in the moment via conceptual combination. This project addresses the need for a first-person, experiential tool that allows youth to directly engage with and explore this constructive process, thereby translating neuroscientific theory into practical emotional literacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that interactive, visual simulation of conceptual blending—allowing youth to map and combine core affective concepts with personal situational knowledge—will enhance their metacognitive understanding of emotion as a constructed process and increase their self-reported sense of emotional self-efficacy.
proposals.method: We will co-design an interactive web platform with adolescents (ages 14-18). The platform will implement a computational model of conceptual blending, representing core affect as a 2D valence-arousal space (derived from pre-2026 affective science) and situational knowledge as a network of semantic features. Youth will visually manipulate these elements using a drag-and-drop interface to create 'blends.' The system will employ dimensionality reduction algorithms (e.g., t-SNE) to project high-dimensional semantic blends back into the 2D affective space, providing real-time visual feedback on the emotional quality of constructed concepts. Physiological and self-report data will be collected during platform use to assess engagement and state shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, theory-driven tool for emotional literacy that operationalizes the constructionist view of emotion for direct user engagement. It will contribute empirical data on how first-person simulation of conceptual blending influences metacognition and self-regulation. The project will also advance methods for translating functional gradient models of brain organization into interactive educational interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While conceptual blending models exist, their application as a first-person, experiential tool for youth emotional development is novel. The approach moves beyond using such models for researcher simulation or clinician training, placing the constructive process directly in the hands of the adolescent to explore their own emotional landscape.

proposals.title: Simulating Emotion Construction: A Computational Blending Model of Core Affect and Conceptualization
proposals.motivation: The theory of constructed emotion posits that emotions emerge from the dynamic integration of core affect and conceptual knowledge, but the specific combinatorial mechanisms remain underspecified. Computational models of conceptual blending, which formalize how mental spaces combine to create new meaning, offer a powerful, testable framework to simulate this construction process. Grounding this in pre-2026 neuroimaging evidence of functional gradients and whole-brain network interactions can bridge computational theory with the neural architecture of experience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the construction of a discrete emotional episode can be formally modeled as a conceptual blend, where inputs from a core affect space (e.g., arousal, valence) and a situated conceptualization space (e.g., social context, prior experience) are selectively projected and composed to yield a novel, emergent emotional meaning.
proposals.method: We will adapt established computational blending frameworks (e.g., the Divago model or blending-as-analogy in the COGENT architecture) to operate on structured representations derived from empirical data. Input spaces will be defined using feature vectors from physiological and interoceptive measures (core affect) and linguistic/situational descriptors from ecological momentary assessments (conceptual knowledge). The model's generative blends will be compared to human participant reports of emotional experiences in controlled social stress paradigms, using similarity metrics to validate the simulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, mechanistic account of emotion construction, moving beyond correlation to explicit process modeling. It will generate testable predictions for how functional gradients in systems like the periaqueductal grey and default mode network support the blending operation. The project will also demonstrate the utility of conceptual blending theory as a novel computational lens for affective neuroscience.
proposals.novelty: While conceptual blending has been applied to language and creativity, its application to modeling the real-time construction of emotional experience from basic physiological and conceptual processes is novel. This approach uniquely operationalizes Barrett's theoretical framework using specific, pre-existing computational cognitive architectures.

proposals.title: Implementing a Constructionist Framework of Emotion in Artificial Agents: A Test of Allostatic Gradients and Functional Connectivity
proposals.motivation: Current AI systems lack the rich, context-sensitive, and body-influenced affective processes that characterize biological intelligence, limiting their adaptability and social intelligence. Grounding this challenge in Lisa Feldman Barrett's constructionist theory of emotion—which posits emotions as constructed from more basic, domain-general processes of allostasis and interoception—offers a principled, neuroscientifically-informed path forward. This research aims to translate this biological framework into a computational architecture, moving beyond simplistic emotion classifiers to create systems that generate emotion-like states functionally.
proposals.hypothesis: An artificial agent equipped with a computational model of allostatic prediction and visceromotor control, implemented via functional connectivity gradients that integrate simulated interoceptive signals with exteroceptive context, will exhibit adaptive, emotion-like behavioral dynamics that are more flexible and context-appropriate than agents using traditional categorical emotion modules.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep reinforcement learning agent with a dual-pathway architecture. A core 'interoceptive' network, inspired by gradients in the periaqueductal grey and functional connectivity between isocortex and cerebellum, will maintain a dynamic, low-dimensional representation of simulated physiological state (e.g., energy cost, arousal). This allostatic signal will be integrated with exteroceptive sensory processing via attention-based gating, influencing policy generation and value estimation. The agent will be tested in multi-context environments requiring social stress (modeled on anticipatory threat paradigms) and effortful decision-making, where we will analyze the emergence of functional, context-specific 'emotional' behaviors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for implementing a constructionist, brain-inspired model of affect in AI, offering concrete algorithms for allostasis and interoceptive inference. It will generate testable predictions about how functional connectivity gradients support affective flexibility, potentially informing both affective neuroscience and the development of more robust, socially-aware artificial agents.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct translation of the constructionist theory's core mechanisms—allostatic gradients and domain-general functional connectivity—into a working AI model, moving beyond correlational mapping of brain activity to implementing its proposed computational functions. It integrates the physiological basis of affect (central to Barrett's work) with modern deep learning in a way not previously attempted.

proposals.title: Capturing Affective Quanta: A Single-Molecule-Inspired Framework for Modeling the Real-Time Construction of Micro-Expressions
proposals.motivation: The theory of constructed emotion posits that emotional episodes are assembled in real-time from more basic, domain-general ingredients. However, the mechanistic 'construction' process at the sub-second, physiological level remains a black box. Current micro-expression research often focuses on categorical recognition, missing the stochastic, dynamic integration of subtle physiological events that may constitute the building blocks of an emotional instance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that micro-expressions are constructed from discrete, stochastic 'affective quanta'—brief, sub-perceptual activations across multiple physiological channels—and that the temporal dynamics and functional connectivity gradients between these channels predict the emergence and intensity of a perceived micro-expression.
proposals.method: We will record high-temporal-resolution (1kHz EMG, 500Hz pupillometry, 100Hz facial thermal imaging) physiological data from human participants during an anticipatory social stress paradigm. Using time-series decomposition and point-process analysis, we will identify stochastic, millisecond-scale events in each channel. We will then apply dynamic functional connectivity and gradient mapping techniques (e.g., Laplacian Eigenmaps) to model how these events are integrated over time. A recurrent neural network (RNN) architecture will be trained to predict the onset and type of micro-expression (coded by independent raters from high-speed video) from the evolving multi-channel physiological state.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic, process-based model of micro-expression generation, directly testing a core tenet of constructionist theory. It will shift the field from a focus on static categorization to a dynamic understanding of affective assembly, offering new quantitative tools for studying emotion as a continuous, constructed process.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its conceptual analogy to single-molecule biophysics, treating transient physiological events as stochastic building blocks, and in its technical application of functional gradient mapping to model the real-time integration of these events across the body and brain's affective systems.

proposals.title: Functional Gradients of Automatic Regulation: Mapping Unconscious Emotion Regulation in the Human Brain-Body System
proposals.motivation: Dominant models of emotion regulation (e.g., Gross, 1998) emphasize deliberate, conscious strategies like reappraisal. However, Lisa Feldman Barrett's theory of constructed emotion and interoceptive inference suggests regulation is a fundamental, ongoing process of allostasis, much of which occurs automatically. Pre-2026, the neural and physiological signatures of automatic, unconscious regulation remain poorly characterized, limiting our understanding of adaptive emotional functioning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that automatic emotion regulation is supported by a specific functional gradient within the brain's allostatic/interoceptive systems, anchored by the periaqueductal grey (PAG) and visceromotor cortex, and that the efficacy of this automatic regulation can be predicted by the alignment of neural activity gradients with peripheral physiological patterns.
proposals.method: We will recruit human participants (N=80) to undergo fMRI and continuous physiology (heart rate, skin conductance, respiration) recording during an adapted social stress task (e.g., Maastricht Acute Stress Test) designed to minimize opportunity for conscious regulation. Using pre-2026 methods, we will apply connectome gradient mapping (e.g., Margulies et al., 2016) to resting-state and task-based data to identify principal gradients of functional organization in the PAG, brainstem, and visceromotor cortex. We will then use multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and functional connectivity to test for distinct spatial patterns of neural-physiological coupling during stress anticipation versus recovery, operationalizing automatic regulation as the return to physiological baseline without explicit instruction.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first functional gradient-based map of the neural architecture supporting automatic emotion regulation, directly testing a core prediction of the theory of constructed emotion. It will offer a novel, systems-level functional account of how the brain unconsciously regulates the body during stress, moving beyond region-centric models and linking macroscale gradients to real-time physiological dynamics.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond simply documenting that regulation can be unconscious; it uses the novel framework of functional gradients to characterize the *organizing principle* of the automatic regulatory system and tests its efficacy via neural-physiological alignment, a hypothesis not yet addressed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Dyadic functional connectivity gradients as a neural signature of interpersonal emotion regulation
proposals.motivation: Lisa Feldman Barrett's theory of constructed emotion posits that emotions emerge from predictive, interoceptive, and exteroceptive processes within the brain. While prior work has mapped intrapersonal functional gradients within single brains, real-world emotion regulation is profoundly social. A critical gap exists in understanding how two brains interact as a coupled system to co-construct and co-regulate affective states, moving beyond the individual as the unit of analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that successful interpersonal emotion regulation in a dyad will be characterized by the emergence of a shared, low-dimensional functional architecture, measurable as correlated gradients of brain activity between partners, particularly in systems supporting allostasis (e.g., visceromotor) and social inference (e.g., default mode).
proposals.method: We will recruit 50 human dyads (e.g., romantic partners) to undergo simultaneous, dual-brain fMRI while participating in a validated social stress task involving mutual support. We will analyze the data using a multi-level gradient mapping approach. First, we will compute whole-brain functional connectivity gradients for each individual using diffusion map embedding. Second, we will develop a novel inter-brain gradient coupling analysis using canonical correlation analysis (CCA) to identify shared low-dimensional subspaces between partners' gradient maps. Third, we will relate the strength of this inter-brain gradient coupling to dyadic physiology (heart rate, respiration synchrony) and behavioral coding of regulatory success.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first neural evidence for a shared, low-dimensional functional architecture underlying interpersonal emotion regulation, directly extending the theory of constructed emotion to the dyadic level. It will offer a novel methodological framework for analyzing two-brain systems, moving beyond simple synchrony measures to characterize the alignment of their intrinsic functional hierarchies.
proposals.novelty: This proposal is novel in applying the established concept of intrapersonal functional gradients to the interpersonal domain, testing the hypothesis that emotion co-regulation involves the alignment of these gradients between brains, rather than just regional synchrony.

proposals.title: Mapping the Computational Structure of Emotional Experience: A Natural Language Processing Analysis of Free-Response Narratives
proposals.motivation: The theory of constructed emotion posits that emotional experiences emerge from the dynamic combination of more basic psychological components (interoceptive, affective, conceptual). However, directly measuring these hypothesized components in naturalistic, self-generated experience reports has been methodologically challenging. Pre-2026, most studies rely on constrained rating scales or predefined categories, which may not capture the richness and idiosyncrasy of lived experience as described in natural language.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying unsupervised natural language processing to free-response emotion narratives will reveal latent semantic dimensions that correspond to the hypothesized construction components of core affect and conceptualization, and that the dynamic interplay of these dimensions will predict self-reported emotional intensity and category.
proposals.method: We will collect extensive free-response narratives of emotional experiences from a large, diverse sample of human participants. Using pre-2026 NLP methods, we will first apply topic modeling (e.g., Latent Dirichlet Allocation) and word embedding techniques (e.g., GloVe) to identify recurrent semantic themes. We will then employ a combination of dictionary-based approaches (e.g., LIWC for affective tone) and distributional semantic models to derive continuous feature vectors for each narrative. Finally, we will use dimensionality reduction techniques (e.g., t-SNE, UMAP) to map the high-dimensional semantic space, testing whether the resulting gradients correspond to hypothesized construction dimensions like valence, arousal, and sociality.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, data-driven computational model of the semantic architecture of emotional experience, directly testing a core prediction of constructionist theory. It will generate a new, publicly available corpus of richly annotated emotional narratives and establish an NLP pipeline for quantifying construction components from free text, moving beyond static category labels.
proposals.novelty: While NLP has been used for sentiment analysis, its application to directly model the component processes proposed by constructionist theory—treating free-response narratives as a direct window into the combinatorial process of emotion construction—is novel. This approach bridges computational linguistics and affective neuroscience in a theory-driven manner.

proposals.title: Constructed Emotion as a Predictor of Social Choice: A Functional Gradient and Interoceptive Inference Account
proposals.motivation: The theory of constructed emotion posits that emotions are not innate, universal categories but are created in the moment by core brain systems to give meaning to sensory inputs and guide action. A critical, untested prediction is that the same physiological and neural ingredients can be configured into different emotional experiences depending on the situated conceptualization of a social context. Behavioral economic games provide a powerful, well-controlled paradigm to test this, as they create specific, quantifiable social contexts (e.g., trust, reciprocity, fairness) that should systematically shape emotional construction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that participants' moment-to-moment conceptualizations of a social partner's intentions during economic games will predict their emotional experiences and subsequent decisions, and that these conceptualizations will be associated with specific, whole-brain functional connectivity gradients linking interoceptive (e.g., anterior insula, periaqueductal grey) and conceptual (e.g., default mode network) systems.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 human participants to complete a multi-round, multi-game fMRI session involving the Trust Game, Ultimatum Game, and Dictator Game with alleged human partners. After key trials, participants will provide granular experience reports using the Emotion Circumplex. We will analyze whole-brain fMRI data using gradient mapping techniques (e.g., diffusion map embedding) to identify low-dimensional manifolds of functional connectivity. We will then test whether the position of a participant's brain activity along these gradients during the decision period predicts their subsequent emotional report and game choice, using hierarchical Bayesian models that treat conceptualization as a latent variable inferred from behavior, physiology (heart rate, skin conductance), and neural gradients.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct neuro-computational evidence linking the construction of emotion to concrete social decisions within a formal economic framework. It will advance the field by moving beyond correlating brain regions with emotion categories to modeling how functional brain architectures support the situational meaning-making that drives choice. It will also offer a novel, mechanistic interpretation of BOLD signal changes in social decision-making paradigms as reflecting the metabolic costs of interoceptive inference and concept construction.
proposals.novelty: While economic games are standard in social neuroscience, applying the constructed emotion framework and gradient-based connectivity analysis to model the *process* of emotional meaning-making *as it drives decisions* is novel. This integrates Dr. Barrett's work on interoception, functional gradients, and construction with the precise experimental control of behavioral economics to test a core, causal prediction of the theory.

proposals.title: Constructing Shared Affect: A Hyperscanning Investigation of Inter-Brain Functional Gradients in Naturalistic Social Dyads
proposals.motivation: The theory of constructed emotion posits that emotions emerge from predictive, context-sensitive brain-body interactions. However, most supporting neuroimaging evidence comes from isolated individuals in artificial settings, failing to capture the dynamic, reciprocal nature of real-world social emotion construction. Understanding how brains interact to create shared affective meaning is a critical, unresolved challenge for affective and social neuroscience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during naturalistic social interaction, the emergence of shared affective states will be reflected in the alignment of functional connectivity gradients between dyad members' brains, particularly within networks supporting interoception (e.g., salience/ventral attention) and allostasis, and that this alignment will be predicted by physiological synchrony.
proposals.method: We will use functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) hyperscanning to record brain activity from 50 human dyads during a naturalistic, emotionally evocative cooperative task. We will apply gradient mapping techniques, analogous to those used in Barrett's prior work on isocortical organization, to characterize each participant's whole-brain functional geometry. We will then compute inter-brain gradient similarity using cross-correlation and dynamic time warping algorithms. Simultaneously, we will collect continuous peripheral physiology (heart rate, electrodermal activity) to measure body-to-brain coupling within and between individuals.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct test of constructed emotion principles in interacting dyads, moving beyond single-brain correlates. It will establish a novel methodological framework for quantifying inter-brain functional geometry and its relationship to shared experience. The findings will bridge the gap between individual-level predictive processing models and the social reality of emotion.
proposals.novelty: While prior hyperscanning studies have examined signal correlations, applying functional gradient analysis to inter-brain dynamics is a novel approach that captures the high-dimensional, organizational principles of brain activity during interaction. Furthermore, integrating this with physiological measures tests a core, but previously unexamined, multi-system prediction of the constructed emotion framework in a social context.

proposals.title: Mapping Core Network Gradients to Constructed Emotion Categories: A Predictive Modeling Approach
proposals.motivation: A core tenet of the theory of constructed emotion is that emotions are categories constructed in the moment by core brain networks. While prior work has mapped how instances relate to network activity, the inverse problem—predicting the specific emotion category from a given network state—remains untested. Solving this inverse problem is critical for validating the theory and moving from correlational to predictive models of emotional experience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatial gradients of activity across the interoceptive, control, and salience networks, particularly within the periaqueductal grey and cortical functional gradients, will contain sufficient information to predict the specific emotion category (e.g., anger, awe) a participant will report constructing.
proposals.method: We will collect high-resolution 7T fMRI data from human participants during an emotion induction task designed to evoke a range of categories. Using pre-2026 methods, we will extract multi-voxel activity patterns and functional connectivity gradients from the core networks. A machine learning model (e.g., a gradient-boosted classifier or a regularized logistic regression) will be trained to predict the self-reported emotion category from these neural features, with rigorous cross-validation to assess generalizability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, predictive test of the constructed emotion framework's central claim, moving beyond description to prediction. It will establish a methodological framework for decoding psychological categories from network states, bridging affective neuroscience and computational psychiatry.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard analytical direction in neuroimaging, predicting the psychological category from the network pattern rather than explaining neural activity with the category label. It uniquely integrates the study of network gradients, physiological states (via PAG), and predictive modeling to test a foundational hypothesis of constructionist theory.

proposals.title: Functional Gradients of Emotional Inability: Linking Whole-Brain Connectivity Patterns to Deficits in Constructing Emotional Experience
proposals.motivation: Research on emotional intelligence (EI) has focused on high performers, leaving a critical gap in understanding the neural and physiological mechanisms underlying deficits in emotional information processing. From the perspective of the theory of constructed emotion, such deficits may reflect a failure to efficiently integrate interoceptive and exteroceptive information to construct situated emotional concepts. This project aims to characterize the functional brain organization associated with these deficits, moving beyond trait-based EI scores to a mechanistic, brain-based account.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individual differences in emotional inability will be associated with atypical functional connectivity gradients, particularly in networks supporting interoception (e.g., salience network) and concept generation (e.g., default mode network), and that these neural patterns will correspond to dysregulated physiological responses during emotional challenges.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 human participants stratified by low scores on the Mayer-Salovey-Caruso Emotional Intelligence Test (MSCEIT). Participants will undergo resting-state and task-based fMRI (anticipatory social stress paradigm) while physiological measures (heart rate, skin conductance, respiration) are recorded. Using pre-2026 methods, we will compute whole-brain functional connectivity gradients (e.g., diffusion map embedding) to identify low-dimensional manifolds of brain organization. We will test for correspondence between these neural gradients, physiological reactivity gradients, and behavioral measures of emotional inability using canonical correlation analysis and predictive modeling (e.g., ridge regression).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first functional connectivity gradient map of emotional inability, linking macroscale brain organization to body-budget regulation and concept construction failures. It will advance the theory of constructed emotion by specifying how neural architecture constrains emotional capacity, moving the field from a focus on performance to a focus on mechanism.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the dominant focus from high EI to its deficit, applying state-of-the-art gradient analyses to a problem typically studied with discrete region-of-interest approaches, and explicitly tests a constructionist, whole-brain account of emotional inability.

proposals.title: Social Prediction Gradients: How Positive Social Outcomes Sculpt the Functional Architecture of Emotional Intelligence
proposals.motivation: The dominant neurobiological models of emotional intelligence (EI) focus on how individual differences in brain function predict social success. This unidirectional framework neglects the powerful, reciprocal possibility that positive social experiences themselves act as a training signal for the brain, actively shaping the functional architecture that supports EI. Grounded in Lisa Feldman Barrett's theory of constructed emotion and predictive processing, this study investigates how the brain's predictive models of the social world are updated by rewarding social outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that repeated, positive social feedback will induce measurable shifts in the functional connectivity gradients of key predictive and interoceptive networks (e.g., default mode, salience, and limbic circuits), and that the magnitude of these shifts will correlate with behavioral improvements in emotion granularity and social prediction accuracy.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 human participants for a longitudinal fMRI study. Participants will complete a validated social prediction task in the scanner at baseline and after a 6-week intervention. The intervention group will engage in structured, positive social interactions with trained confederates, designed to provide consistent, rewarding feedback. Using pre-2026 methods, we will analyze changes in whole-brain functional connectivity gradients (Laplacian eigenmaps) to map shifts in cortical and subcortical organization. We will specifically test for gradient changes in the periaqueductal grey and anterior insula, linking them to physiological regulation (heart rate variability) and behavioral measures of emotion differentiation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic, longitudinal evidence for how positive social outcomes sculpt the brain's predictive models, moving beyond correlational accounts of EI. It will offer a novel functional account of how social experience can alter interoceptive and allostatic gradients, directly testing a core tenet of the theory of constructed emotion. The findings could inform targeted interventions for social-emotional learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links brain states to social stress, this study uniquely inverts the causal arrow to test how rewarding social *outcomes* drive neuroplasticity in predictive and interoceptive systems. It combines gradient-based neuroimaging with a controlled social learning paradigm to directly observe experience-dependent changes in the functional architecture of EI.

proposals.title: A Predictive Processing Framework for the Integration of Body Ownership, Affective Experience, and Exteroceptive Perception
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on body ownership (e.g., rubber hand illusion), emotion (e.g., constructed emotion theory), and perception largely operates in separate silos, despite shared reliance on interoceptive and exteroceptive signals. This fragmentation limits a unified understanding of how the brain creates a coherent, affectively rich experience of the world. A framework that formally integrates these domains is needed to generate novel, testable hypotheses about their neural and physiological basis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a common predictive processing architecture, implemented via functional connectivity gradients spanning interoceptive (e.g., periaqueductal grey, insula) and exteroceptive (e.g., sensory, parietal) systems, underlies the dynamic construction of body ownership, emotional experience, and perceptual inference.
proposals.method: We will recruit human participants for a multimodal neuroimaging study combining fMRI and peripheral physiology. Using a modified rubber hand illusion paradigm with emotionally valenced stimuli, we will induce states of altered body ownership under varying affective contexts. We will apply gradient mapping techniques (e.g., diffusion map embedding) to resting-state and task-based connectivity data to identify low-dimensional manifolds that capture the integration of interoceptive and exteroceptive systems. Hypothesis-driven analyses will test for shifts in these gradients and their correlation with physiology and subjective reports.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal, neurobiologically grounded model linking predictive coding across body ownership, emotion, and perception. It will generate a novel set of testable predictions about the role of specific neural gradients and physiological states in constructing subjective experience. The findings will advance the interpretation of BOLD signals by situating them within an integrated metabolic and predictive account.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit formal integration of three distinct predictive frameworks into a single model and the use of connectivity gradient analysis to test its neuroanatomical basis, moving beyond correlational approaches to reveal organizing principles of experience.

proposals.title: Bayesian Model Comparison of Interoceptive Generative Models Reveals Gradients of Emotional Experience
proposals.motivation: A core tenet of the theory of constructed emotion is that emotional categories are not biologically given but are constructed from more basic systems of interoception and allostasis. Pre-2026 neuroimaging has identified gradients of functional organization in the brain and body-related physiology, but a formal computational framework for how these continuous signals give rise to discrete emotional experiences is lacking. This gap limits our ability to test competing hypotheses about the generative models the brain uses to predict and categorize interoceptive signals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct emotional experiences (e.g., anxiety, excitement, calm) correspond to distinct interoceptive generative models, and that Bayesian model comparison of these models, applied to combined neural (e.g., periaqueductal grey, insula) and peripheral physiological data, will outperform traditional classification approaches in predicting subjective emotional reports.
proposals.method: We will collect high-field (7T) fMRI and continuous peripheral physiology (heart rate, skin conductance, respiration) from human participants during an anticipatory social stress task and other interoceptive challenges. We will construct competing generative models (e.g., predictive coding architectures) that formalize different hypotheses about how the brain predicts bodily states. Using variational Bayesian model comparison (e.g., Bayesian Model Selection/Reduction) and hierarchical Bayesian inference, we will compute model evidence for each emotional episode and identify the neural and physiological feature gradients (using methods like Laplacian eigenmaps) that best distinguish between models of different emotional categories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, testable computational account of emotional construction grounded in interoceptive inference. It will move the field beyond correlational brain-behavior maps to a mechanistic understanding of how specific generative models are selected. The results will offer a novel methodological bridge between the continuous, graded nature of neural-physiological organization and the categorical nature of reported experience.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian models of interoception exist, applying formal model comparison to classify emotional *types* based on competing generative hypotheses is novel. Furthermore, integrating this approach with recent findings on functional connectivity gradients provides a unique, multi-scale framework for understanding emotional construction.

proposals.title: Socially Situated Interoception: A Computational Test of How Cultural Priors Shape Emotional Experience via Functional Brain Gradients
proposals.motivation: Interoceptive inference models, a cornerstone of the theory of constructed emotion, explain how the brain predicts and categorizes bodily sensations to construct emotional experiences. However, these models have been largely tested at the individual level, neglecting how shared cultural knowledge and social context provide the priors that shape these predictive processes. This gap limits our understanding of the profound social and cultural variation in emotional life documented in anthropology and psychology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an individual's culturally acquired priors for interpreting interoceptive signals will systematically modulate the functional gradients connecting visceral control centers (e.g., periaqueductal grey) to transmodal cortical systems (e.g., default mode network), and that this modulation will predict the phenomenological content of socially situated emotional experiences.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 human participants from distinct cultural cohorts (e.g., East Asian and Western European backgrounds). Using a combination of naturalistic video stimuli depicting socially stressful scenarios and controlled interoceptive attention tasks during 7T fMRI, we will map functional connectivity gradients between deep brainstem nuclei and the isocortex. We will employ hierarchical Bayesian modeling (e.g., predictive coding or active inference architectures) to formalize how participant-reported cultural priors (measured via ethnographic interviews and standardized surveys) influence the precision weighting of interoceptive prediction errors. The model's estimated prior strengths will be linked to gradient topography and trial-by-trial experience reports.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first neurocomputational model formally integrating cultural priors into interoceptive inference, moving beyond individualistic accounts. It will generate novel, testable predictions about how social context gets 'under the skin' to shape brain function and subjective experience, bridging cultural psychology, affective neuroscience, and computational psychiatry.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has mapped functional gradients and studied interoception, no study has yet used gradient-based neuroimaging to test a formal computational model of how culturally transmitted priors alter the core interoceptive inference machinery proposed by constructionist theories.

proposals.title: Mapping the Functional Topography of Affective Experience: A Gradient-Based Test of Pleasure-Activation Independence
proposals.motivation: Core affect models, such as the circumplex, posit that pleasure (valence) and activation (arousal) are independent, orthogonal dimensions. However, this structural independence is a theoretical assumption that has not been rigorously tested at the level of large-scale brain organization. Pre-2026 work by Barrett and others shows that functional brain organization is best described by continuous, low-dimensional gradients, not discrete modules, raising the hypothesis that these affective dimensions may also be represented in a correlated, non-orthogonal manner within these cortical gradients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the neural representations of pleasure and activation are not independent but are instead correlated, manifesting as a shared, low-dimensional gradient within the brain's intrinsic functional architecture, particularly across transmodal association cortices.
proposals.method: We will collect fMRI data from 100 human participants during a task eliciting varied affective states (e.g., viewing IAPS images, recalling autobiographical events) and during rest. Using representational similarity analysis (RSA), we will construct neural representational dissimilarity matrices (RDMs) for pleasure and activation ratings. We will then apply diffusion map embedding, a non-linear manifold learning technique, to identify the primary low-dimensional gradients in these RDMs and in resting-state functional connectivity data. The key test is whether the first principal gradient of the pleasure RDM is significantly correlated (via Spearman's rank correlation) with the first gradient of the activation RDM and with the brain's principal functional connectivity gradient (e.g., the sensorimotor-to-transmodal axis).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, brain-based test of a foundational assumption in affective science, moving beyond self-report correlations. It will contribute a functional account of how core affective features are embedded within the brain's intrinsic geometry, potentially reconciling dimensional and constructionist theories of emotion. The findings will also offer a new framework for interpreting BOLD signal changes in affective neuroimaging by mapping them to a shared affective manifold.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has mapped valence and arousal to separate brain regions, this study is the first to explicitly test their neural independence using the non-linear gradient frameworks central to Barrett's recent research, moving from a question of regional localization to one of representational geometry within continuous brain space.

proposals.title: Cultural and Contextual Variation in the Dimensional Structure of Affective Experience: A Multi-Method Test of Universality
proposals.motivation: The two-dimensional model of affect (valence and arousal/activation) is a dominant framework in affective neuroscience and psychology, often treated as a universal structure of human emotional experience. However, this model is largely derived from Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, and Democratic (WEIRD) populations. Lisa Feldman Barrett's theory of constructed emotion suggests that emotional categories and their underlying physiological patterns are culturally and contextually situated, raising a critical hypothesis: the neural and experiential architecture of affect may not be universally captured by a fixed two-dimensional space.
proposals.hypothesis: The neural and experiential gradients corresponding to pleasure (valence) and activation will show significant cross-cultural and cross-contextual variation in their organization, alignment, and explanatory power, challenging the universality of a fixed two-dimensional model.
proposals.method: We will recruit participants from three distinct cultural groups (e.g., US, Japan, and a small-scale, non-WEIRD society) and expose them to a standardized set of affect-eliciting stimuli (e.g., images, sounds, social scenarios) in controlled and naturalistic contexts. Functional MRI data will be analyzed using gradient mapping techniques (e.g., diffusion map embedding) on whole-brain functional connectivity patterns to identify low-dimensional neural manifolds. We will test for cross-cultural and cross-contextual correspondence in these neural gradients and their relationship to continuous self-reports of valence and activation, employing Procrustes analysis and cross-validated variance partitioning.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, multi-method test of the universality of the two-dimensional affective model at both neural and experiential levels. It will advance our understanding of how culture and context shape the fundamental building blocks of affective life, offering critical data for theories of constructed emotion. The findings will challenge assumptions in affective computing, cross-cultural psychology, and neuroimaging research that rely on a fixed dimensional structure.
proposals.novelty: While cultural differences in emotion labels are documented, this proposal directly tests for variation in the core *dimensional structure* of affect itself, using Barrett's own methodological framework (gradient mapping) to interrogate a foundational assumption of her theoretical opposition. It moves beyond self-report to examine the cultural consistency of the underlying neural architecture.

proposals.title: Latent Classes in Affective Space: A Factor Mixture Modeling Approach to Uncover Subgroups in Dimensional Experience
proposals.motivation: The theory of constructed emotion posits that emotional experiences emerge from the interplay of core affect and conceptualization, suggesting a dimensional structure of affect. However, pre-2026 research often treats this dimensional space as homogeneous across individuals, potentially obscuring meaningful subgroups with distinct patterns of affective construction. Identifying such latent classes is crucial for understanding individual differences in emotional life and their neurobiological underpinnings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying factor mixture modeling (FMM) to high-dimensional self-report and physiological data will reveal distinct latent classes of individuals who systematically differ in their organization of core affective experience within a shared dimensional space.
proposals.method: We will recruit a large, diverse sample of human participants to complete intensive experience sampling and laboratory-based affective tasks, collecting multivariate self-report and peripheral physiology (e.g., heart rate, skin conductance). We will apply factor mixture modeling, a latent variable technique that combines confirmatory factor analysis (to model the dimensional structure) with latent class analysis (to identify subgroups). Model selection will be guided by fit indices (e.g., BIC, aBIC, BLRT) to determine the optimal number of classes and factor structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, person-centered statistical account of affective dimensionality, moving beyond population averages. It will generate testable predictions about how different latent classes may correspond to distinct functional connectivity gradients in the brain (e.g., in the isocortex or periaqueductal grey) and patterns of physiological regulation, directly linking statistical subgroups to neurobiological mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While factor analysis and latent class analysis are used separately, their integrated application via FMM to model affective construction is novel. This approach tests the non-obvious proposition that the brain's functional architecture for constructing emotion may manifest in statistically identifiable subgroups within a population, rather than a single, universal affective space.

proposals.title: Mapping the Functional Topography of Micro-Moment Affect: A Multi-Modal Investigation of Sub-Second Affective Dynamics
proposals.motivation: The theory of constructed emotion posits that emotional episodes are built from more fundamental, dynamic core affective changes. However, the functional architecture of affect at the sub-second timescale—the hypothesized building blocks of experience—remains poorly characterized. This gap limits our understanding of how momentary physiological shifts and neural patterns give rise to the phenomenology of feeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that micro-moment affective changes (on the order of 100-500ms) are not monolithic states but are themselves structured along continuous, low-dimensional functional gradients that integrate interoceptive and exteroceptive information, and that these gradients can be reliably mapped in human brainstem and cortical regions.
proposals.method: We will collect high-temporal-resolution (7T fMRI) and high-density physiological (ECG, EDA, respiration) data from 50 human participants during a novel task presenting rapidly shifting affective stimuli. Using a combination of (1) time-resolved functional connectivity analysis (e.g., sliding window correlations) and (2) manifold learning techniques (diffusion map embedding) applied to both neural and peripheral physiology time-series, we will identify low-dimensional gradients characterizing micro-moment states. We will test for correspondence between neural and physiological gradients using canonical correlation analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first functional map of affective dynamics at the sub-second scale, directly testing a core prediction of constructionist theory. It will establish a methodological framework for studying micro-genesis of emotion and offer a new interpretation for rapid BOLD signal fluctuations in terms of core affective processes.
proposals.novelty: While gradient mapping has been applied to static brain architecture, applying these techniques to dynamic, multi-modal second-scale data to reveal the functional structure of momentary affect is a novel synthesis of Dr. Barrett's conceptual and methodological work.

proposals.title: Mapping the Affective Landscape: A Computational Inverse Solution for Predicting Event Elicitors from Whole-Brain Functional Gradients
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in affective neuroscience is the inverse inference problem: moving from observed brain states to the specific experiences that caused them. While Lisa Feldman Barrett's theory of constructed emotion posits that affective states emerge from whole-brain functional architectures, current methods primarily map forward from stimuli to brain activity. A robust inverse model is needed to understand how the brain's functional geometry constrains the types of events that can elicit specific affective moments, bridging brain structure and lived experience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the high-dimensional functional gradients of the human brain, which capture continuous, low-dimensional representations of functional organization, contain sufficient information to probabilistically predict the categories of real-world events (e.g., social evaluation, physical threat, reward pursuit) that are likely to elicit a given coordinate in a core affective space defined by valence and arousal.
proposals.method: We will collect fMRI and peripheral physiology data from human participants during a rich, naturalistic event-viewing task featuring validated video clips spanning diverse event categories. Using pre-2026 methods, we will compute whole-brain functional connectivity gradients from resting-state and task data. We will then train a probabilistic inverse model (e.g., a Hierarchical Gaussian Process or a structured variational autoencoder) to learn the mapping from a low-dimensional affective space (derived from participant ratings and physiology) and individual gradient patterns to the posterior distribution over event categories. Model performance will be tested on held-out event clips and validated with a separate hypothesis-driven task.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first computational framework for performing inverse inference from whole-brain functional architecture to event categories, directly testing a key prediction of constructionist theory. It will move the field beyond forward encoding models, offering a novel tool for generating testable hypotheses about the real-world causes of brain states and individual differences in emotional reactivity.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its explicit focus on solving the inverse problem for affective experience using whole-brain functional gradients as the structural prior, rather than decoding from localized activity patterns. It integrates Barrett's gradient-based framework with computational inverse modeling in a way not previously attempted.

proposals.title: Mapping Affective Space to Cognitive-Behavioral Gradients: A Functional Connectivity and Computational Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: A core tenet of the theory of constructed emotion is that affective states are not discrete entities but high-dimensional, situated representations that prepare the body for action. However, a precise, brain-based mapping of how continuous positions in this affective space predict specific cognitive and behavioral outcomes remains a critical gap. This project directly addresses this dual problem, moving beyond correlation to test a functional account of how affect scaffolds cognition and action.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an individual's momentary position within a whole-brain functional connectivity gradient space, anchored in affectively relevant systems like the periaqueductal grey and visceromotor cortex, will systematically predict their performance on cognitive tasks and their behavioral tendencies in a social stress paradigm.
proposals.method: We will collect multimodal data from 120 human participants during an anticipatory social stress task (TSST) and a subsequent cognitive battery. Using 7T fMRI, we will derive whole-brain functional connectivity gradients (using diffusion map embedding) to characterize each participant's affective state. We will then employ canonical correlation analysis (CCA) and predictive modeling (e.g., ridge regression) to map these neural gradients to a behavioral space comprising cognitive performance metrics (e.g., executive function, attention) and detailed behavioral coding from the TSST. Physiological measures (heart rate, skin conductance) will be integrated as validating dimensions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic model of the affect-cognition-behavior interface, demonstrating how the brain's functional architecture supports the translation of affective state into thought and action. It will extend gradient-based analyses of isocortex and cerebellum into a domain of central interest to affective science, offering a new methodological framework for studying individual differences in emotional reactivity and regulation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has mapped functional gradients or linked affect to behavior, this study is novel in using whole-brain, affectively anchored gradients as predictive features for a multivariate cognitive-behavioral space, directly testing a constructionist, dimensional account of emotion's functional role.

proposals.title: Unconscious Core Affect: Mapping Subcortical-Cortical Gradients of Pleasure and Arousal During Masked Stimulus Processing
proposals.motivation: A core tenet of the theory of constructed emotion is that core affective states (pleasure-displeasure, arousal) are fundamental to allostasis and form the basis of conscious emotional experiences. However, it remains an open question whether these elemental processes can be triggered and influence neural activity without entering conscious awareness. Resolving this is critical for understanding the architecture of affect and its role in perception, decision-making, and psychopathology, challenging purely cortical, conscious-centric models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that masked, subliminal stimuli varying in affective tone (e.g., pleasant/unpleasant, high/low arousal) will evoke differentiable patterns of neural activity in subcortical allostatic/interoceptive hubs (e.g., periaqueductal grey, hypothalamus) and will systematically shift functional connectivity gradients in the isocortex, even in the absence of conscious stimulus recognition or self-reported affect.
proposals.method: We will recruit human participants (N=60) for a combined fMRI and peripheral physiology study. Using a backward masking paradigm with carefully calibrated affective images, we will present stimuli below and above conscious detection thresholds. We will employ hypothesis-driven general linear modeling to identify neural activity in subcortical regions. Crucially, we will apply connectopic gradient mapping (e.g., Laplacian eigenmaps) to resting-state and task-based functional connectivity data to test if unconscious affective stimuli induce systematic, low-dimensional shifts in cortical organization, linking subcortical activity to large-scale cortical patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct neural evidence for unconscious core affective processes, grounding a key theoretical claim in systems neuroscience. It will advance methods for studying unconscious phenomena by integrating subcortical BOLD signal interpretation with cortical gradient analyses. The findings will refine models of allostasis and predictive processing by delineating the neural precursors to conscious feeling.
proposals.novelty: While prior work shows subliminal emotional processing, no study has explicitly tested for core affect—conceptualized as elemental pleasure and arousal—using Barrett's theoretical framework and the novel methodological integration of subcortical allostatic mapping with whole-brain functional connectivity gradients.

proposals.title: Neural and Physiological Gradients of Multi-Causal Emotional Episodes
proposals.motivation: The theory of constructed emotion posits that emotional episodes are constructed from core systems, not triggered by dedicated circuits. However, it remains untested whether and how the brain represents the perception of multiple concurrent causes for a single emotional episode. This is crucial for understanding real-world emotional complexity, where feelings often arise from a confluence of internal and external factors, challenging simple, single-cause models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that complex emotional episodes attributed to multiple concurrent causes will be associated with distinct, spatially distributed functional connectivity gradients across the brain's interoceptive and control networks, and that these neural patterns will correlate with specific physiological response profiles.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 human participants to undergo a novel social-evaluative stress task while undergoing 7T fMRI and continuous physiology recording (ECG, EDA, respiration). The task incorporates simultaneous, orthogonal manipulations of social threat and physical discomfort (e.g., cold pressor). Post-scan, participants will use a dynamic causal attribution tool to rate the perceived contribution of each manipulated cause and other self-generated causes to their experienced emotion. We will apply gradient mapping techniques (e.g., diffusion map embedding) to resting-state and task-based functional connectivity data to identify low-dimensional manifolds. We will test if attribution complexity (number and strength of causes) maps onto specific gradients and correlates with multivariate patterns of physiological activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first neural and physiological account of multi-causal emotional construction, directly testing a key prediction of constructionist theory. It will advance methods for studying emotional complexity by integrating gradient neuroimaging, physiological patterning, and subjective causal attribution, moving beyond categorical or valence-arousal models.
proposals.novelty: While past work has studied mixed emotions or context effects, no study has explicitly manipulated and measured the neural correlates of perceiving *multiple concurrent causes* for a single emotional episode, linking this perception to whole-brain functional organization gradients and embodied physiology.

proposals.title: The Frequency and Functional Architecture of Culturally Scripted Emotional Episodes: A Multi-Modal Neuroimaging Investigation
proposals.motivation: A core debate in affective science concerns the frequency of prototypical emotional episodes (e.g., discrete anger, fear). The theory of constructed emotion posits such episodes are not biologically fixed but are constructed from more basic core systems, suggesting their occurrence might be less frequent than assumed. However, this prediction conflicts with the pervasive presence of culturally learned emotional scripts that may guide perception and action, potentially making script-consistent episodes frequent. Resolving this tension is critical for understanding the real-world prevalence of emotional categories.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that emotional episodes consistent with high-level cultural scripts will be frequently observed in behavior and physiology, and that their neural correlates will be best described by graded, whole-brain functional architectures rather than discrete, category-specific activations.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 human participants to complete a novel task involving viewing and reacting to complex social scenarios designed to evoke script-consistent and script-inconsistent emotional responses. We will collect continuous self-report, peripheral physiology (heart rate, skin conductance), and 7T fMRI data. Using representational similarity analysis (RSA) and gradient mapping techniques (as in Goulas et al., 2021), we will test whether neural patterns for script-consistent episodes are more similar to each other across individuals and are organized along functional gradients spanning the cerebrum, cerebellum, and hippocampus, linking to physiological and subjective features.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first multi-modal test of the frequency and neural architecture of culturally scripted emotional episodes. It will advance the theory of constructed emotion by clarifying when and how cultural knowledge shapes emotional experiences into common categories. The findings will also offer a novel functional account linking large-scale brain gradients to the physiological and experiential dimensions of frequently occurring emotional events.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has mapped brain gradients or studied constructed emotion in isolation, this proposal uniquely integrates these lines of inquiry to test a non-obvious prediction: that frequent, script-consistent emotional episodes are supported by a graded, domain-general functional architecture, not rare, dedicated neural circuits.

proposals.title: Dissociating Appraisal from Affect: A Test of Constructed Emotion Theory Using Interoceptive Perturbation and Functional Connectivity Gradients
proposals.motivation: A central debate in affective science concerns whether cognitive appraisal is a necessary antecedent for emotional episodes. Pre-2026, appraisal theories dominate, yet Lisa Feldman Barrett's constructed emotion theory posits that emotions emerge from predictive interoceptive-allostatic processes, not necessarily from discrete appraisals. Resolving this debate is critical for understanding the fundamental architecture of emotional experience and its neural basis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted interoceptive perturbations (e.g., visceral, cardiorespiratory) will reliably evoke self-reported emotional episodes and corresponding whole-brain activity patterns, even when explicit cognitive appraisal of an external stimulus is minimized or absent.
proposals.method: We will recruit human participants (N=120) for a within-subjects fMRI study. Using a respiratory load task and intravenous isoproterenol infusions (pre-2026 established methods), we will induce interoceptive arousal while participants view emotionally neutral stimuli. We will employ hypothesis-driven psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analysis to test for altered connectivity between the periaqueductal grey (PAG) and cortical allostatic/interoceptive networks. Data-driven functional connectivity gradient analyses (e.g., diffusion map embedding) will be used to characterize whole-brain representational shifts during perturbation versus control conditions, independent of stimulus appraisal.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct empirical test of a core tenet of constructed emotion theory, moving beyond correlation to demonstrate causal links between interoception and emotion. It will advance methods for studying affective physiology by integrating perturbation-based fMRI with connectivity gradient analysis, offering a novel functional account of emotional episode generation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links interoception to affect, this study uniquely tests the *necessity* of appraisal by directly inducing emotional episodes via subcortical-allostatic circuit perturbation and analyzing whole-brain functional organization shifts, rather than relying on stimulus-evoked appraisal paradigms.

proposals.title: Discrete Boundaries in a Continuous Space: A Gradient-Based Test of Categorical Emotion Structure
proposals.motivation: A core debate in affective science is whether emotions like anger, fear, and happiness are discrete natural kinds with sharp boundaries or are constructed from core affect dimensions in a fuzzy, context-dependent manner. Pre-2026, Barrett's conceptual act theory strongly favors the latter, constructionist view, but direct neural tests of categorical boundaries using continuous, whole-brain organizational principles remain underexplored. Resolving this is critical for models of emotion generation, taxonomy, and clinical intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that if basic emotion categories are discrete, their associated neural patterns will occupy distinct, non-overlapping regions in a low-dimensional functional manifold, with sharp transitions (high gradient magnitudes) at category boundaries, rather than forming a smooth continuum.
proposals.method: We will collect high-resolution 7T fMRI data from human participants (N=50) during cued anticipation of and exposure to validated stimuli for six basic emotions and neutral states, capturing autonomic physiology. Using representational similarity analysis, we will construct neural pattern dissimilarity matrices. We will then apply diffusion map embedding to derive a continuous functional connectivity gradient space spanning the whole brain, particularly integrating isocortical, cerebellar, and hippocampal systems. We will test for categorical boundaries by analyzing the distribution of emotion-specific patterns within this gradient space, using a support vector machine classifier and quantifying the sharpness of decision boundaries between categories compared to within-category variance.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, whole-brain test of categorical emotion theory using state-of-the-art gradient mapping, moving beyond localized activation patterns. It will directly contribute to the debate on the fundamental structure of emotion by linking it to the brain's intrinsic functional architecture. The findings will refine models of how categorical experiences emerge from, or are imposed upon, continuous neural and physiological signals.
proposals.novelty: While gradient mapping is established for macro-scale brain organization, its application to test the discreteness of psychological categories like emotion is novel. The approach uniquely combines high-field neuroimaging, physiology, and manifold learning to directly quantify the 'sharpness' of emotion category boundaries in a continuous neural space, offering a more rigorous test than traditional univariate methods.

proposals.title: Mapping the Neural Architecture of Emotion: A Multivariate Pattern Analysis of Distributed Functional Gradients
proposals.motivation: A core debate in affective neuroscience is whether emotions are realized by dedicated, category-specific neural circuits or emerge from more general, distributed patterns of brain activity. Pre-2026 work, including Barrett's conceptual act theory and research on functional gradients, suggests the latter, but direct evidence linking specific, whole-brain multivariate patterns to distinct emotional experiences remains limited. This study directly tests this prediction using advanced pattern classification on high-resolution neuroimaging data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct emotional experiences (e.g., anger, fear, sadness) will be characterized by unique, spatially distributed patterns of whole-brain functional connectivity and activity gradients, and that these patterns can be reliably decoded using multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA).
proposals.method: We will collect high-resolution 7T fMRI data from human participants during well-validated emotion induction tasks. Using preprocessed data, we will compute whole-brain functional connectivity matrices and estimate functional gradients (e.g., using diffusion map embedding) to capture low-dimensional spatial organization. We will then apply machine learning classifiers, specifically linear Support Vector Machines (SVMs) and regularized logistic regression, to decode emotional categories from these multivariate patterns. Analyses will focus on cross-validated classification accuracy and feature importance mapping to identify contributing brain regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, data-driven test of the distributed, pattern-based account of emotion. It will move beyond localization debates by demonstrating whether emotions are identifiable from the brain's functional architecture. The results will offer a novel methodological framework for studying emotion as a whole-brain state defined by gradients and connectivity patterns.
proposals.novelty: While MVPA has been used in perception, its application to decode emotional *experiences* from whole-brain functional gradients—integrating Barrett's theoretical framework with cutting-edge connectome-based methods—is novel. This moves beyond analyzing isolated regions or networks to test how the brain's intrinsic spatial organization supports affective phenomena.

proposals.title: Mapping the Cultural Construction of Affective Experience: An Ethnographic and Neurophenomenological Investigation
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in understanding the brain basis of emotion, a critical gap remains in how cultural context actively constructs emotional experiences in real-world settings. Pre-2026 work, including Barrett's theory of constructed emotion, posits that emotions are not universal biological categories but are constructed from more basic, domain-general processes. However, most supporting evidence comes from controlled lab studies or broad cross-cultural surveys, lacking the deep, situated analysis of how cultural learning and social interaction shape the functional architecture of affect.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that culturally specific practices for interpreting and regulating interoceptive and exteroceptive sensations will be reflected in both the granularity of participants' reported experiences and the spatial gradients of functional connectivity within networks supporting interoception (e.g., salience network) and conceptualization (default mode network).
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-method design integrating cultural anthropology and cognitive neuroscience. First, immersive ethnographic fieldwork will be conducted with two culturally distinct participant groups to document local emotion concepts, scripts, and regulation practices. Second, human participants from these groups will complete a neuroimaging session involving tasks that elicit anticipatory social stress and interoceptive attention, similar to Barrett's prior work. We will analyze fMRI data using connectivity gradient mapping (e.g., diffusion map embedding) to characterize the principal axes of functional organization in key regions like the periaqueductal grey, anterior insula, and medial prefrontal cortex. These neural gradients will be statistically related to ethnographic data and experiential granularity measures derived from in-depth interviews.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, mechanistic account of how cultural learning shapes the brain's functional architecture for constructing affective experience. It will generate a rich dataset linking fine-grained cultural analysis with brain organization, moving beyond simple group comparisons. The work will offer a new methodological template for studying brain-culture interactions, directly testing core tenets of the theory of constructed emotion in ecologically valid contexts.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct integration of prolonged ethnographic fieldwork with state-of-the-art neural gradient analysis, treating cultural context not as a categorical variable but as a dynamic process to be modeled alongside brain function. This moves beyond post-hoc interpretation of group differences to a hypothesis-driven test of how cultural construction manifests in brain organization.

proposals.title: Mapping Population-Level Emotion Concept Networks: A Gradient-Based Analysis of Large-Scale Linguistic Corpora
proposals.motivation: The theory of constructed emotion posits that emotion concepts are culturally situated and learned, serving as predictive models for making meaning of interoceptive and sensory inputs. Pre-2026 research has primarily validated this at the individual or small-group neurobiological level (e.g., in the periaqueductal grey or cortical gradients), leaving a critical gap in understanding how these concepts are structured at the population scale where culture emerges. A large-scale, data-driven map of cultural emotion concept networks is needed to bridge neurobiological and sociocultural levels of analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that population-level linguistic data will reveal a high-dimensional, continuous semantic space (or gradient) for emotion concepts, where the proximity and relationships between concepts reflect shared functional roles in social prediction and body budgeting, rather than discrete, universal categories.
proposals.method: We will analyze a large-scale, timestamped corpus of English-language social media posts (e.g., Twitter/X or Reddit data archives up to 2025) using pre-trained transformer-based language models (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa). We will extract contextual embeddings for a seed set of emotion words and their co-occurrence networks. Dimensionality reduction techniques (e.g., UMAP, t-SNE) and manifold learning will be applied to identify the primary gradients structuring this semantic space. We will validate these gradients by correlating them with demographic metadata and by testing their predictive power for the functional context (e.g., social, somatic) of sentences in held-out data.
