proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, data-driven model of cultural emotion concept networks, directly informing the population-level structure assumed by the theory of constructed emotion. It will create a public resource for linking cultural linguistic patterns to individual neurobiological findings (e.g., functional connectivity gradients) and generate novel, testable hypotheses about cultural variation and temporal evolution in emotion concepts.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used neuroimaging to study brain gradients for emotion and NLP for sentiment analysis, this proposal uniquely applies gradient-based manifold learning—a method central to Feldman Barrett's recent neuroimaging research—to population-level linguistic data to directly model the cultural architecture of emotion concepts predicted by her theory.

proposals.title: Predicting Situated Emotional Language from Whole-Brain Functional Gradients: An Inverse Inference Approach
proposals.motivation: Traditional neuroimaging studies of emotion often assume a fixed, universal mapping between brain activity patterns and predefined emotion categories (e.g., fear, anger). This approach is at odds with the theory of constructed emotion, which posits that emotional experiences are constructed in a situated, context-dependent manner, and that the emotion labels individuals use are predictions based on interoceptive and exteroceptive cues. A critical inverse problem remains: can we predict the specific emotion label a person will use from their brain activity, without presupposing a fixed neural fingerprint for that label?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an individual's chosen emotion label for a subjective experience can be predicted from the spatial configuration of whole-brain functional connectivity gradients, and that this predictive relationship will be more accurate when models incorporate physiological (e.g., cardiovascular) signals as additional predictors, consistent with interoceptive inference accounts.
proposals.method: We will collect fMRI and peripheral physiology (heart rate, skin conductance) data from human participants during an autobiographical emotion recall task, where they subsequently label their experienced emotion using a free-response method. We will derive whole-brain functional connectivity gradients from the fMRI data using diffusion map embedding. A multi-view machine learning architecture (e.g., a kernel-based or deep canonical correlation analysis framework) will be trained to integrate the primary gradient dimensions (capturing macroscale neural organization) with physiological features. This integrated model will perform multi-class classification to predict the free-response emotion labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for testing constructionist theories by moving from forward (brain-to-category) to inverse (brain-to-label) inference. It will demonstrate how large-scale brain organization (gradients) and bodily signals jointly constrain the conceptual categorization of experience. The findings will offer a new paradigm for studying individual and cultural variation in emotional reporting.
proposals.novelty: Unlike studies that test for neural correlates of pre-defined categories, this proposal treats the emotion label itself as a variable to be predicted from a brain state, operationalizing a core tenet of constructionist theory. It innovatively combines the analysis of functional gradients—a recent method for describing brain organization—with interoceptive data in a predictive model of subjective report.

proposals.title: Disrupted Interoceptive-Affect Integration: Mapping Functional Connectivity Gradients in Major Depressive Disorder and Generalized Anxiety Disorder
proposals.motivation: The theory of constructed emotion posits that emotions emerge from the brain's predictive integration of interoceptive signals with sensory and conceptual information. Psychiatric disorders like Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) and Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD) are characterized by profound alterations in emotional experience, yet the specific neural mechanisms of this construction failure remain poorly specified. Grounded in pre-2026 work on functional gradients and the periaqueductal grey's role in stress, this proposal seeks to identify disorder-specific disruptions in the brain's functional architecture that support emotion construction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that MDD and GAD will exhibit distinct, diagnosable alterations in the functional connectivity gradients linking interoceptive (e.g., brainstem, insula, anterior cingulate) and transmodal conceptual (e.g., default mode network) systems, and that the magnitude of gradient distortion will correlate with specific clinical profiles of anhedonia and anxious arousal.
proposals.method: We will collect resting-state and task-based fMRI (including an anticipatory social stress paradigm) from three matched cohorts: participants with MDD, GAD, and healthy controls. Using pre-2026 methods, we will compute whole-brain functional connectivity gradients via diffusion map embedding to characterize the primary axes of functional organization. We will specifically test for alterations in the gradient spanning sensorimotor-to-transmodal cortex and a subcortical-cortical gradient anchored by the periaqueductal grey. Statistical analyses will compare gradient scores between groups and correlate them with physiology (heart rate, skin conductance) and clinical symptom scales.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a mechanistic, network-level account of how emotion construction breaks down in two prevalent disorders, moving beyond regional activation deficits. It will generate testable neurobiological markers that distinguish depressive from anxious pathophysiology, offering a framework for developing more targeted interventions. Furthermore, it will extend gradient mapping techniques to clinical populations and deeper brain structures central to interoception.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has mapped gradients in healthy brains, applying this framework to characterize clinical emotion dysregulation as a disruption in core functional axes is novel. Simultaneously modeling cortical gradients and brainstem/cerebellar contributions to interoceptive-affective integration provides a more comprehensive test of constructionist theory in psychopathology than previous studies.

proposals.title: Culturally-Grounded Gradients of Affect: A Multi-Method Test of Situated Somatic and Social Processes
proposals.motivation: Dominant neurobiological models of emotion are often built on Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, and Democratic (WEIRD) participant samples and a priori, universalist constructs, risking cultural imposition. This limits our understanding of how affective and somatic processes are dynamically constructed within specific cultural ontologies of mind, body, and relationship. A framework that first grounds its models in local experience is needed to test hypotheses about common and variable patterns of human brain-body-world interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that culturally coherent experiential tasks, derived from ethnographic models, will reveal both shared and distinct spatial gradients of functional connectivity in interoceptive-allostatic (e.g., periaqueductal grey, anterior insula) and social-cognitive networks, corresponding to culturally situated patterns of physiological engagement and meaning-making.
proposals.method: Phase 1: In-depth ethnographic fieldwork (participant observation, linguistic analysis) in two contrasting cultural sites will generate grounded models of affective/somatic experience. Phase 2: We will design culturally tailored experimental tasks (e.g., evocative social scenarios, interoceptive attention tasks) based on these models. A coordinated multi-site study will collect synchronized functional MRI (to map whole-brain and cerebellar functional connectivity gradients), continuous physiology (heart rate, skin conductance), and experiential reports from human participants during these tasks. Analysis will use hypothesis-driven gradient mapping and pattern regression to link neural, physiological, and ethnographic data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, process-based framework for cross-cultural affective science that avoids lexical and conceptual fallacies. It will generate the first datasets linking culturally derived experiential models to multi-level measures of brain, body, and behavior, testing core tenets of constructionist theories. The results will clarify how functional gradients support situated meaning-making.
proposals.novelty: The approach reverses the standard paradigm by using ethnography to design experiments, rather than testing pre-defined constructs across cultures. It integrates gradient-based neuroimaging with thick cultural description to compare processes, not categories.

proposals.title: Mapping the Constructed Landscape of Affect: A Cross-Cultural Psychometric Framework for Emotion Using a Standardized Situational Array
proposals.motivation: A core debate in affective science, pre-2026, is whether emotions are universal biological categories or culturally constructed concepts. Current methods often rely on translating English emotion words, which confuses shared physiological triggers with culturally variable conceptual frameworks. A culture-neutral elicitation tool is needed to systematically separate these influences and map how different societies partition affective experience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a standardized, multimodal array of core affective situations will reveal a high-dimensional, graded space of physiological and situational features that different cultures partition into discrete emotion categories in divergent, yet predictable, ways, consistent with the theory of constructed emotion.
proposals.method: We will develop a core array of 50-100 short, multimodal vignettes (video, audio, text) depicting fundamental social and physiological situations (e.g., goal conflict, loss, novelty, exertion). This array will be deployed in 8-10 culturally diverse field sites. Researchers will use it to document the local emotion lexicon, associated narratives, bodily feelings (via standardized body maps), and social appraisals. Data will be analyzed using psychometric network analysis and functional connectivity gradient mapping techniques (e.g., diffusion map embedding) to model the high-dimensional space of features and plot how cultural categories ('emotion concepts') partition this space.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first systematic, stimulus-grounded map of cross-cultural emotion construction, directly testing universalist vs. constructionist accounts. It will yield a novel methodological framework for culture-neutral affective elicitation and generate a publicly available dataset linking situational features, physiology, and conceptualization across cultures.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in adapting the rigorous, stimulus-anchored methodology of cross-linguistic color categorization to the domain of emotion, moving beyond lexical translation studies. It operationalizes constructionist theory by using gradient mapping to visualize how cultural concepts carve up a shared affective landscape.

proposals.title: Temporal Dynamics of Affect: Testing the Primacy of Core Affect over Categorical Labeling in Emotional Experience
proposals.motivation: A core debate in affective neuroscience concerns whether emotional categories (e.g., fear, anger) are the fundamental building blocks of experience or are constructed post-hoc. Lisa Feldman Barrett's theory of constructed emotion posits that categorization is a constructive act. This proposal directly tests a key temporal prediction of this view: that a state of core affective arousal (characterized by gradients of autonomic and neural activity) precedes and is distinct from the later cognitive process of labeling that state with a discrete emotion word.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during an emotionally evocative event, (1) neural and physiological activity reflecting core affective features (e.g., arousal, valence gradients) will peak earlier in time than activity in language- and categorization-related brain regions, and (2) the intensity and pattern of this early core affective activity will better predict subsequent subjective intensity reports than the specific categorical label chosen by the participant.
proposals.method: We will recruit human participants (N=60) to undergo a social stress paradigm (e.g., anticipatory public speaking) while simultaneous high-resolution 7T fMRI and peripheral physiology (ECG, EDA, respiration) are recorded. We will employ a combination of established and novel analytical techniques: (1) Time-resolved multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) to identify the onset and peak of neural patterns associated with core affect (using gradients in subcortical and interoceptive networks like the periaqueductal grey and anterior insula) versus semantic categorization (using language network regions). (2) Functional connectivity gradient analysis across the scan session to map how whole-brain network configurations shift from affect-laden to categorization-laden states. (3) Trial-by-trial modeling to dissociate physiological arousal (body) from subsequent verbal label selection.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, multimodal test of the temporal sequence between affective experience and categorization. It will generate a novel functional account of how the brain transitions from a state of embodied core affect to a categorized emotional experience, directly informing interpretations of BOLD signal dynamics. The findings will critically advance the constructed emotion theory by providing mechanistic, temporal evidence for its core tenets.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has correlated physiology with emotion categories, this study uniquely tests the critical temporal prediction that core affect precedes labeling. The integration of time-resolved neural decoding with autonomic gradients and connectivity dynamics offers a novel methodological framework for studying the real-time construction of experience.

proposals.title: Resolving the Emotion Paradox: A Functionalist Account of Folk Psychological Categories and Their Neural Gradients
proposals.motivation: The 'emotion paradox'—the tension between the discrete, universal categories of folk psychology and the variable, context-dependent neural patterns found in scientific studies—has long been framed as a central puzzle for affective neuroscience. This framing, however, may itself be a conceptual artifact of reifying folk categories as natural kinds. Resolving this conceptual impasse is critical for advancing a scientifically valid ontology of emotional experience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the so-called emotion paradox arises from a category error: folk psychological emotion categories (e.g., 'anger,' 'fear') are functional summaries of highly variable instances, not scientific hypotheses about brain states. Therefore, the neural correlates of these categories will manifest as broad, overlapping functional gradients within large-scale brain networks, not as discrete, dedicated neural modules.
proposals.method: We will recruit human participants (N=120) to experience and label emotional states during fMRI. Using a hypothesis-driven, multi-voxel pattern analysis framework, we will model neural activity not as localized 'emotion centers' but as continuous functional gradients across the cortex, cerebellum, and subcortex (e.g., periaqueductal grey). We will apply dimensionality reduction techniques (e.g., diffusion map embedding) to these whole-brain patterns to identify low-dimensional manifolds that capture the principal axes of neural variation. We will then test the correspondence between these neural gradients and the continuous physiological (e.g., cardiovascular, respiratory) and subjective report gradients collected simultaneously.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, data-driven demonstration that the folk psychological illusion of discrete emotions is not a problem for neuroscience to solve, but a feature of functional categorization to explain. It will advance the field by offering a formal, gradient-based model that bridges brain, body, and experience without reifying folk categories, thereby clarifying the scientific ontology of emotion.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described functional gradients in the brain, this proposal uniquely applies this framework directly to deconstruct the emotion paradox, treating folk categories as the explanandum rather than the explanans. It integrates Barrett's conceptual work on constructed emotion with cutting-edge, pre-2026 gradient mapping methods to test a radical functionalist hypothesis.

proposals.title: Testing the Perceptual Reality of Emotion Categories: A Speeded Categorization and Priming Study
proposals.motivation: A core debate in affective science concerns whether emotions are natural kinds with dedicated neural substrates or are constructed via the situated application of conceptual knowledge. Pre-2026, Barrett's theory of constructed emotion posits that emotional experiences arise from the categorization of core affective states using emotion concepts. Directly testing the mechanistic role of concept application in real-time emotion perception remains a critical challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the application of emotion concepts during perceptual tasks will show behavioral signatures (e.g., priming effects, reaction time gradients) analogous to those found in object categorization, supporting the view that emotion perception is a conceptual act rather than a direct readout of sensory input.
proposals.method: We will adapt classic paradigms from visual object recognition (e.g., rapid serial visual presentation with speeded categorization, masked priming) to emotion stimuli. Participants will categorize dynamic, ambiguous facial and bodily expressions under time pressure. We will analyze behavioral data using drift-diffusion modeling to separate decisional from perceptual processes. Functional MRI will be acquired during the task, and we will apply gradient mapping techniques (e.g., diffusion map embedding) to the resulting whole-brain connectivity patterns to characterize the continuous, low-dimensional neural manifolds underlying concept application, linking them to physiological and subjective report measures.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel behavioral and neural evidence for the conceptual nature of emotion perception. It will bridge cognitive neuroscience methods for studying object concepts with affective science, offering a formal computational account of how emotion concepts are applied in real time. The gradient analysis will move beyond discrete localization to show how concept application emerges from whole-brain functional architecture.
proposals.novelty: While priming studies exist in emotion research, applying the rigorous speeded categorization and formal modeling frameworks from high-level object recognition to emotion is novel. Combining these behavioral paradigms with whole-brain connectivity gradient analysis to test a constructionist hypothesis represents a significant methodological and theoretical integration.

proposals.title: Mapping the Conceptual Topography of Emotion Language: A Natural Language Processing Analysis of Semantic Structure and Contextual Variation
proposals.motivation: According to the theory of constructed emotion, emotional experiences are not biologically hardwired but are constructed from more basic domain-general processes, with emotion concepts playing a crucial role. A core, testable prediction is that emotion concepts should exhibit graded, context-dependent structure in natural language use, reflecting their functional role in guiding action and making meaning. Pre-2026, this prediction has not been systematically tested at scale using computational linguistic methods that can capture the high-dimensional semantic structure and contextual embedding of these concepts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that emotion concepts in natural language corpora will not form discrete, bounded categories but will instead be organized along continuous, interpretable semantic gradients (e.g., from high to low arousal, from socially embedded to physically embedded), and that the position of a given concept along these gradients will systematically shift with linguistic context (e.g., social vs. physical threat narratives).
proposals.method: We will apply state-of-the-art (pre-2026) NLP models, specifically contextual embedding architectures like BERT, to large, diverse text corpora (e.g., news, fiction, social media). For a predefined set of emotion words, we will extract their contextual embeddings across millions of instances. We will then use dimensionality reduction techniques (e.g., UMAP, t-SNE) and correlational methods to identify the primary semantic gradients structuring the emotion concept space. Finally, we will use hierarchical regression models to test how contextual features (extracted via topic modeling) predict a concept's position on these gradients.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, data-driven map of the semantic landscape of emotion concepts as they are actually used, offering empirical evidence for or against their proposed graded and constructed nature. It will generate novel, quantifiable hypotheses about the functional organization of the conceptual system that can later be tested against neural data, such as the functional connectivity gradients observed in the researcher's prior work.
proposals.novelty: While NLP has been used for sentiment analysis, applying gradient-based and contextual embedding analysis specifically to test core predictions of constructionist theory about the *structure* of emotion concepts is novel. This bridges a critical gap between high-level theory and computationally tractable, large-scale linguistic evidence.

proposals.title: Mapping the Social Construction of Affect: A Large-Scale Computational Analysis of Emotion Concept Gradients in Public Discourse
proposals.motivation: The theory of constructed emotion posits that emotions are not universal biological categories but concepts constructed from interoceptive and exteroceptive sensations in a given context. While studied in individual brains, how these concepts scale to shape collective affective reality in public discourse remains untested. Analyzing large-scale media data can reveal how populations collectively apply and negotiate emotion concepts, influencing social coordination and public sentiment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that collective emotion narratives in public discourse will not map onto discrete, universal emotion categories but will instead be organized along continuous, context-dependent functional gradients (e.g., valence-arousal, sociality, certainty) that reflect the population-level construction of affective meaning.
proposals.method: We will analyze temporal corpora from Twitter (2019-2021) and news media (GDELT Project) using pre-2026 natural language processing. We will employ transformer-based language models (e.g., BERT) fine-tuned for affective and semantic similarity to generate high-dimensional vector representations of text segments. Dimensionality reduction (UMAP, t-SNE) and gradient mapping techniques, analogous to those used for neural connectivity, will be applied to these representations to identify dominant, low-dimensional gradients structuring the affective semantic space. We will test how these gradients shift in response to major societal events.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, data-driven test of constructed emotion theory at the population level, moving beyond the individual brain. It will establish a novel methodological framework for quantifying the functional architecture of collective affective meaning and offer insights into how shared emotion concepts dynamically shape public discourse and potentially collective behavior.
proposals.novelty: This project uniquely applies the gradient-mapping framework from Dr. Barrett's neuroimaging work to the domain of large-scale cultural text analysis, operationalizing 'emotion concepts' as measurable structures in public language rather than as fixed neural entities.

proposals.title: Mapping the Social Construction of Emotion: A Large-Scale Computational Model of Population-Level Emotion Concept Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Lisa Feldman Barrett's theory of constructed emotion posits that emotions are not universal biological categories but concepts constructed by the brain, shaped by social learning and cultural context. However, the mechanisms by which these emotion concepts and norms propagate, stabilize, or change across large populations remain poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to model collective emotional phenomena, such as societal mood shifts or the spread of new emotional vocabularies, which are critical for public health and social cohesion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spread and evolution of emotion concepts within a population can be modeled as a dynamic process on a functional gradient space, where individual conceptual representations (informed by interoceptive and social cues) interact and align through social communication, leading to measurable population-level attractor states and transitions.
proposals.method: We will recruit a large, diverse online participant pool (N > 10,000) to complete tasks assessing their personal emotion concept spaces (via similarity judgments and body sensation mapping) and social networks. Using pre-2026 methods, we will apply manifold learning (e.g., UMAP, diffusion map) to derive low-dimensional functional gradients representing individual conceptual spaces. These individual gradient spaces will serve as nodes in an agent-based model, where interaction rules are informed by social network analysis and theories of conceptual alignment. The model will be validated against longitudinal data on real-world emotion word usage trends scraped from social media corpora (2010-2025).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale computational framework for the population-level dynamics of constructed emotion, directly testing a core tenet of the theory. It will generate novel, testable predictions about how social network structure influences collective emotional understanding and offer a new methodological bridge between individual neurocognitive gradients and macro-scale cultural phenomena.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has mapped functional gradients in the individual brain and studied social contagion of simple behaviors, this proposal uniquely integrates these approaches to model the complex, high-dimensional dynamics of socially constructed concepts at population scale, moving beyond static, brain-bound accounts of emotion.

proposals.title: Functional gradients of facial movement: A test of the social communication hypothesis using naturalistic dyadic fMRI
proposals.motivation: Dominant emotion theories treat facial movements as readouts of internal affective states, but their predictive validity is low. A constructionist framework suggests facial actions are tools for regulating social interactions, not passive expressions. Testing this requires moving beyond static, decontextualized stimuli to measure how facial movements function in real-time social exchange.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the neural and physiological patterns associated with facial movements will vary systematically along a functional gradient from affiliative to antagonistic social goals, rather than clustering by discrete emotion categories.
proposals.method: We will use naturalistic dyadic fMRI (N=60 human participants in pairs) during a structured social negotiation task. Facial movements will be coded via automated facial action unit (AU) detection (OpenFace 2.0). We will apply connectome gradient mapping (as in Margulies et al., 2016) to whole-brain BOLD data to identify low-dimensional representations of neural activity. Using representational similarity analysis, we will test if patterns of neural (gradient space) and peripheral physiology (heart rate, skin conductance) similarity are better modeled by the social function of the facial movement (coded by independent raters) than by traditional emotion category labels.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a novel functional account of facial movement neurophysiology, directly testing constructionist predictions against basic emotion theory. It will advance methods for studying real-time social interaction in the scanner and link facial action to broader brain-body computational principles of allostasis and interoception.
proposals.novelty: This study integrates naturalistic social fMRI with connectome gradient analysis to test a functional, goal-based hypothesis of facial movement, moving beyond the long-standing debate focused on categorical emotion signaling. It explicitly treats the body and brain as an integrated system supporting social action.

proposals.title: Mapping the Functional Gradients of Affective Experience: A Continuous, Situated Alternative to Categorical Models
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in affective neuroscience treats emotion categories (e.g., fear, anger) as natural kinds with dedicated neural substrates, yet meta-analyses consistently fail to find consistent, specific brain correlates. This categorical approach may obscure the fundamental, continuous nature of how the brain constructs affective experiences. A more precise, mechanistic account requires shifting the unit of analysis from categories to the continuous, low-dimensional dynamics that integrate interoceptive, exteroceptive, and conceptual information in a situated context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that whole-brain functional connectivity gradients, particularly those anchored in the periaqueductal grey and other visceromotor regions, will capture continuous dimensions of affective experience (e.g., core affect) more reliably than discrete emotion categories, and that the situated context (e.g., social stress) will systematically modulate these gradient patterns.
proposals.method: We will collect high-resolution 7T fMRI and peripheral physiology (heart rate, skin conductance) from human participants during a novel task inducing graded, contextually situated social-evaluative threat. Using pre-2026 methods, we will compute whole-brain functional connectivity matrices and apply diffusion map embedding to identify low-dimensional gradients. We will then use representational similarity analysis (RSA) to test whether these gradient configurations correspond more strongly to continuous ratings of valence and arousal (and situated action tendencies) than to categorical emotion labels. Analyses will specifically test for correspondence between neural gradients in the periaqueductal grey and physiological reactivity gradients.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, data-driven framework for moving beyond emotion categories in neuroscience. It will demonstrate how continuous functional brain organization supports situated affective phenomena, directly testing a core prediction of the theory of constructed emotion. The findings will offer a new methodological template for analyzing affective experience that integrates neural, physiological, and behavioral dimensions without reifying categories.
proposals.novelty: While gradient analyses exist in systems neuroscience, their application to deconstruct emotion categories by linking them to continuous physiological states and situated contexts is novel. This approach moves beyond simply comparing categories to explicitly modeling the continuous, whole-brain architectural basis of affective experience.

proposals.title: Predicting Social Outcomes from Facial Action Unit Gradients Without Emotion Inference: A Constructed and Functional Account
proposals.motivation: Dominant approaches in affective computing and social neuroscience infer hidden emotional states from facial movements, a practice challenged by the theory of constructed emotion. This inference is problematic as it reifies folk-psychological categories, ignores context, and may be unnecessary for predicting behaviorally relevant outcomes. A system that bypasses state inference to directly link facial action to social consequences would offer a more parsimonious and biologically plausible model of social communication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that continuous, low-dimensional gradients of facial muscle movements (Action Units), measured in social contexts, can directly predict an observer's functional behavioral and physiological responses (e.g., approach/avoidance tendencies, autonomic reactivity) more accurately than models that first categorize these movements into discrete emotional states.
proposals.method: We will collect high-fidelity facial electromyography (fEMG) and video from human participants during controlled social stress paradigms (e.g., Trier Social Stress Test). Using pre-2026 computer vision (OpenFace 2.0) to extract time-series of Action Unit intensities, we will apply dimensionality reduction (t-SNE, UMAP) to derive continuous movement gradients. These gradients, along with contextual features, will be used to train machine learning models (e.g., Gaussian Process Regression, recurrent neural networks) to predict concurrent and subsequent behavioral choices and physiological measures (e.g., skin conductance, heart rate) from interacting partners, explicitly excluding emotion labels as training targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, data-driven framework for studying facial behavior grounded in functionalism and construction, moving beyond essentialist emotion inference. It will demonstrate a viable alternative for affective computing systems and generate testable hypotheses about how the brain might use sensory-motor summaries (gradients) to guide action in social perception, aligning with recent work on functional connectivity gradients in the brain.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally testing the necessity of emotion inference as an explanatory middleman, directly modeling the link between facial action gradients and functional outcomes—a shift from 'what emotion is it?' to 'what does this facial action accomplish?'—using state-of-the-art gradient and predictive modeling approaches from pre-2026 neuroscience.

proposals.title: Functional Coding of Facial Movements: A Test of Social-Ecological Hypotheses in the Human Brain
proposals.motivation: Traditional emotion perception research often codes facial movements using pre-defined emotion categories (e.g., 'anger,' 'fear'), which Barrett's theory of constructed emotion suggests are not biologically given. This approach conflates the physical signal with its inferred meaning. A functional coding scheme, derived from behavioral ecology's approach to animal communication, would instead categorize facial configurations by their apparent social-contextual function (e.g., affiliation, threat, submission), providing a more mechanistic and hypothesis-driven framework for studying the brain basis of social perception.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patterns of whole-brain functional connectivity, particularly gradients in the isocortex and subcortex, will more strongly correlate with and predict socially functional codes for facial movements than with traditional emotion category labels.
proposals.method: We will recruit human participants to view dynamic facial expressions in varied social scenarios. Using Facial Action Coding System (FACS) data, movements will be coded via two schemes: (1) traditional emotion labels and (2) novel functional codes (e.g., 'reduce social distance,' 'assert dominance'). We will collect fMRI data to measure brain activity and functional connectivity. Using representational similarity analysis (RSA) and gradient mapping techniques (e.g., diffusion map embedding), we will test which coding scheme produces more interpretable and consistent neural representational geometries across the cortex, cerebellum, and subcortical regions like the periaqueductal grey.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, falsifiable framework for studying the brain's role in social communication, moving beyond circular emotion labeling. It will generate concrete hypotheses about how specific functional action patterns map onto well-characterized brain connectivity gradients, advancing a functionalist interpretation of neuroimaging signals in social neuroscience.
proposals.novelty: While behavioral ecology's functional approach is established in animal research, its systematic application to human facial movement coding and direct linkage to whole-brain connectivity gradients is novel. This directly tests a core tenet of constructionist theory—that category meaning is context-dependent—using a rigorous, non-circular coding system.

proposals.title: Modeling the Many-to-One Mapping from Emotional State to Facial Action: A Functionalist and Constructionist Account of Expressive Variability
proposals.motivation: Classical emotion theories posit a one-to-one mapping from internal states to prototypical facial expressions, a view challenged by constructionist accounts like the theory of constructed emotion. Pre-2026 evidence shows high variability in facial movements during emotional episodes, but the computational principles governing this many-to-one mapping remain poorly understood. This study directly addresses the inverse problem: given a specific emotional state (as constructed by the brain), what are the possible facial outputs, and what factors constrain this variability?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the mapping from a constructed emotional state to facial action is not deterministic but is instead a degenerate, many-to-one function, where the specific output is constrained by a gradient of contextual factors including interoceptive state (e.g., autonomic physiology), immediate social goals, and prior learning.
proposals.method: We will recruit human participants (N=120) to undergo controlled social stress induction while high-density facial EMG and autonomic physiology (heart rate, skin conductance) are recorded. Participants' subjective emotional states will be sampled via experience sampling. We will model the data using a variational autoencoder (VAE) architecture to learn a low-dimensional latent space of emotional states from multimodal physiological and self-report data. A conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN) will then be trained to generate the distribution of possible facial EMG patterns (the 'output') given a point in the emotional state latent space, with contextual factors (e.g., social goal condition) as additional conditioning variables.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first computational model of expressive degeneracy, formally demonstrating the many-to-one mapping central to constructionist theory. It will shift the focus from decoding emotion from expression to predicting the range of possible expressions from a state. The findings will offer a new functional account of BOLD signal variability in regions like the periaqueductal grey by linking it to the generative process of context-sensitive action.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has documented variability, this study proactively models it as a fundamental computational principle using state-of-the-art generative neural networks. It inverts the standard 'expression-to-emotion' decoding paradigm to test a core, non-obvious prediction of constructionist theory: that an emotional state is a cause of, but not isomorphic to, a specific facial configuration.

proposals.title: Neural and Physiological Gradients of Unexpressed Emotion: A Functional Connectivity Study of Social Context and Interoception
proposals.motivation: Classical emotion theories posit a strong, obligatory link between felt emotion and facial expression, but everyday experience and cross-cultural data suggest frequent decoupling. Understanding when and why emotions are not expressed is a critical, understudied 'dual problem' essential for a complete functional account of emotional episodes. This directly addresses a core challenge in affective neuroscience: explaining the variable relationship between internal experience and external behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the decoupling of felt emotion from facial expression is a functional, context-sensitive process supported by specific gradients of functional connectivity between interoceptive/visceromotor regions (e.g., periaqueductal grey) and prefrontal regulatory networks, and that this decoupling will be predicted by the social context and an individual's interoceptive sensitivity.
proposals.method: We will recruit 80 human participants to undergo fMRI while experiencing socially evocative stimuli designed to induce strong, self-reported emotional states (e.g., unfairness, embarrassment) under two contexts: 'express' and 'suppress' social feedback. We will measure high-resolution physiology (ECG, skin conductance, facial EMG). Using pre-2026 methods, we will apply connectopic mapping and gradient analysis (e.g., Laplacian eigenmaps) to characterize whole-brain functional connectivity gradients, with a focus on the PAG and isocortical networks. We will test for context-dependent shifts in these gradients and their correlation with physiological arousal and interoceptive accuracy scores.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first functional connectivity map of the brain states associated with unexpressed emotion, moving beyond cataloging expressions to explaining their absence. It will offer a mechanistic, brain-based account of how social context and internal body sensing regulate emotional behavior, directly contributing to the theory of constructed emotion. The data will also inform interpretations of BOLD signal changes in social-emotional paradigms.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied expression suppression as effortful regulation, this proposal investigates the non-occurrence of expression as a potentially default, functional outcome in specific contexts, using cutting-edge gradient analyses to model the underlying whole-brain functional architecture rather than isolated region activity.

proposals.title: Mapping the Continuous Functional Topography of Emotional Experience: A Gradient-Based Account of Holistic Affective Flow
proposals.motivation: Dominant neuroimaging approaches often treat emotions as discrete categories or dimensions, imposing artificial boundaries on subjective experience. This conflicts with the theoretical view, central to the theory of constructed emotion, that emotional episodes emerge from the continuous, holistic interplay of core brain systems. A method to characterize this intrinsic continuity is needed to move beyond state-based models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the neural representation of emotional experience is best captured as a low-dimensional, continuous manifold (or gradient) within the functional architecture of transmodal and visceromotor networks, rather than as discrete, spatially segregated states. This manifold will reflect the smooth integration of interoceptive, conceptual, and exteroceptive information.
proposals.method: We will collect high-resolution 7T fMRI data from human participants during a 30-minute naturalistic viewing task designed to evoke a broad, dynamic flow of affective experience. Applying connectopic gradient mapping (e.g., Laplacian eigenmaps) to the periaqueductal grey, anterior insula, and ventromedial prefrontal cortex, we will quantify principal gradients of functional connectivity. We will then use a hidden Markov model with a high number of states to test if a continuous gradient model provides a superior fit to the neural time series compared to a discrete state model, while simultaneously modeling continuous physiology (heart rate, skin conductance).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal neurobiological model of emotional experience as a continuous, indivisible flow, directly testing a core tenet of constructionist theory. It will advance methods for analyzing naturalistic affective neuroscience data by moving beyond event-related and categorical frameworks, offering a new paradigm for studying individual differences in emotional granularity and dysregulation.
proposals.novelty: While gradient approaches have mapped cognition and perception, applying them to model the *temporal continuity* of a subjective, internal experience like emotion is novel. The integration of connectopic mapping with time-series modeling to directly compare continuous vs. discrete neural accounts of emotion has not been done.

proposals.title: Mapping the Affective Landscape: A Functional Connectivity Gradient Account of Emotional Experience
proposals.motivation: The dominant model of emotion as discrete, universal categories (e.g., anger, fear) is increasingly challenged by meta-analytic and neuroimaging evidence showing high variability and degeneracy in neural correlates. This necessitates a search for organizing principles that better explain the richness and fluidity of emotional phenomena. Grounded in the theory of constructed emotion, this proposal seeks to identify these principles in the brain's intrinsic functional architecture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the continuous, low-dimensional gradients that organize functional connectivity in the isocortex, cerebellum, and hippocampus will systematically map onto the principal dimensions of self-reported affective experience (e.g., valence, arousal, intensity), providing a more parsimonious and biologically plausible account than discrete emotion categories.
proposals.method: We will collect resting-state fMRI and detailed, trial-by-trial experiential reports of affective states from 150 human participants using ecological momentary assessment. Applying diffusion map embedding—a non-linear dimensionality reduction technique used in recent gradient mapping studies—to whole-brain functional connectivity data, we will derive principal connectivity gradients. Using canonical correlation analysis, we will test for robust statistical correspondence between these neural gradients and multivariate experiential dimensions derived from participant reports, controlling for physiological measures like heart rate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, gradient-based neural signature for affective experience that transcends traditional category boundaries. It will directly test a core prediction of constructionist theories by linking the brain's intrinsic, domain-general organizing principles to the phenomenological structure of emotion, moving the field beyond localizationist approaches.
proposals.novelty: While gradients have been mapped to cognitive domains, their specific correspondence with the fine-grained structure of affective experience remains untested. This approach is novel in treating emotion as a high-dimensional, continuous phenomenon to be mapped onto the brain's intrinsic functional geometry, rather than searching for dedicated circuits for predefined categories.

proposals.title: Mapping the Cultural Construction of Emotion: A Multi-Level Study of Functional Connectivity Gradients and Social Network Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Lisa Feldman Barrett's theory of constructed emotion posits that emotions are not universal biological categories but concepts constructed from interoceptive and exteroceptive sensations, shaped by a person's cultural context. However, pre-2026 research has primarily studied these constructions at the individual brain level. A critical gap exists in understanding how these emotion concepts are collectively maintained and transmitted within real-world social systems, which is essential for a complete, multi-scale account of human experience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the functional organization of an individual's brain (specifically, gradients of connectivity in systems like the default mode and salience networks) will show greater alignment with the emotion concept norms of their primary cultural in-group, and that this neural-cultural alignment will be predicted by their position and affective interactions within their local social network.
proposals.method: We will recruit 200 participants from distinct, pre-existing cultural communities (e.g., university clubs, cultural associations). Each participant will undergo resting-state and task-based fMRI to map individual-level functional connectivity gradients (using dimensionality reduction techniques like diffusion map embedding). Simultaneously, we will map their ego-centric social networks and quantify shared emotion concept use via ecological momentary assessment (EMA) of affective language and a novel 'emotion concept alignment' task performed with network ties. We will test our hypothesis using multi-level modeling, treating individuals as nested within communities, and employ network analysis (e.g., exponential random graph models) to relate neural gradients to social interaction patterns.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first neurobiological evidence for how macro-scale cultural norms are instantiated in the meso-scale organization of individual brains via micro-scale social interactions. It will extend the theory of constructed emotion beyond the individual to the social system, offering a novel framework for studying psychopathology, cultural transmission, and social cohesion. It will also pioneer methods for integrating gradient-based neuroimaging with dynamic social network analysis.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied cultural differences in brain function or social networks in isolation, this proposal is novel in its direct, quantitative integration of within-brain functional gradients, quantified social network structure, and real-time, dyadic measures of emotion concept use to explain the emergence of collective affective reality.

ideas.title: A Cross-Cultural 'Periodic Table' of Emotion: Mapping Construction via Culturally-Grounded Psychometrics
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodological solution from cross-linguistic color categorization studies to examine cultural variation in emotion construction. In linguistics, the use of a standardized Munsell color array as a culture-neutral stimulus base allowed for the systematic mapping of different languages' color lexicons, revealing universal and culture-specific patterns. The adapted idea is to develop a core 'array' of standardized emotional stimuli (e.g., multi-modal vignettes depicting social situations, core physiological patterns) that can serve as a common elicitation tool across cultures with divergent emotion concepts and interoceptive practices. Field researchers would use this stimulus set to document the local emotion lexicon, associated narratives, bodily feelings, and social appraisals. The resulting data would enable the construction of a comparative map—analogous to a 'periodic table' for emotion—that plots how different cultures partition the landscape of affective experience, directly testing theories of universalism versus social construction by separating shared physiological triggers from culturally variable conceptual and perceptual frameworks.

ideas.title: Mapping Neural Processes to Experiential Content via a Cognitive 'Periodic Table'
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the historical problem of classifying chemical elements—solved by developing the Periodic Table based on fundamental atomic properties—to the challenge of mapping neural processes to experiential content. The analogous solution was moving from a mere catalog of elements to a predictive system based on core principles (atomic number, electron shells). The adapted idea proposes to identify a set of fundamental, measurable neurobiological 'primitives' (e.g., circuit-temporal dynamics, neuromodulator ratios, oscillation couplings) that serve as the basic components of emotional states. By systematically characterizing how these primitives combine, the research aims to construct a predictive framework that explains how specific neural processes instantiate the distinct properties (valence, arousal, granularity) of subjective emotional experience, moving beyond correlation to a principled, compositional explanation.

ideas.title: A Grounded, Two-Phase Framework for Comparing Culturally-Situated Affective and Somatic Processes
ideas.description: This research first conducts in-depth ethnographic and linguistic work within each target culture to empirically derive local models of affective, bodily, and relational experience, avoiding the imposition of an a priori emotion construct. Using these grounded models, the study then designs culturally coherent experimental and experiential tasks that probe the situational, somatic, and social dynamics highlighted within each framework. These tailored protocols are administered in a coordinated multi-method design across sites, enabling a robust comparison of the processes of meaning-making, interoceptive engagement, and social coordination in response to evocative contexts. The result is a comparison of dynamic affective processes that respects cultural ontology while identifying potential commonalities and variations in human experiential patterning.

ideas.title: A Computational Resource-Allocation Model of Emotion's Regulatory Function
ideas.description: This research program tests a formal model in which emotional states function as adaptive signals for reallocating finite cognitive and metabolic resources among competing internal processes and external goals. It investigates specific trade-offs, such as how anxiety narrows attentional focus at the cost of perceptual breadth, quantifying the 'cost' and 'benefit' in terms of performance metrics and physiological expenditure. The approach combines behavioral tasks, pupillometry, heart rate variability, and computational modeling to derive quantifiable principles of how emotion manages the mind's budget of resources.

ideas.title: Study emotion construction via micro-expressions using single-molecule biophysics techniques
ideas.description: This research adapts the core solution from single-molecule biophysics—observing stochastic, transient states of individual units to understand a system's fundamental mechanics—to the study of emotion construction. In biophysics, techniques like FRET reveal the hidden, brief conformational states of a single enzyme that are masked in bulk assays. Analogously, we scale down the observation of emotion to its putative 'molecular' level: the briefest, often sub-perceptual, activations of the affective system (e.g., a 50ms corrugator muscle twitch, a transient pupillary dilation) that precede and constitute a micro-expression. By using high-speed, multi-modal physiological sensing (EMG, pupillometry, high-frame-rate facial thermal imaging) with millisecond precision, we can capture these stochastic 'affective quanta.' The goal is to model how these discrete, subtle cues are dynamically integrated or 'constructed' over hundreds of milliseconds into a coherent micro-expression and, potentially, a nascent emotional experience, moving beyond the categorization of expressions to understanding their real-time assembly.

ideas.title: Youth-Centric Computational Co-Design of Emotional Experience
ideas.description: This research direction develops interactive, youth-accessible computational platforms based on models of conceptual blending. Instead of using these models primarily for simulation by researchers or clinician training, the platforms are designed for direct use by adolescents and young adults. Youth engage with the system to visually map and blend core affective concepts with personal situational knowledge, simulating the construction of novel emotional experiences in a safe, reflective environment. This process empowers them to understand emotion as a flexible, constructed process they can influence, fostering emotional literacy and self-regulation from a first-person, experiential perspective.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Emotion Construction Landscapes
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecology's Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) to the problem of emotion construction. In ecology, SDMs (like MaxEnt) solve the problem of predicting a species' range by modeling its fundamental niche (internal tolerances) interacting with external environmental variables, framing it as a network of probabilistic constraints. The adapted research idea applies this analogically: treat a specific emotional state (e.g., anger) as analogous to a species, core affect (valence/arousal) as its fundamental physiological niche, and active conceptualizations (e.g., appraisals of blame, goal obstruction) as the dynamic external 'environment'. Network analysis methods can then model the interaction topology and map the multi-dimensional 'construction landscape,' identifying stable attractor basins for discrete emotions and the pathways between them, thereby formalizing the dynamic construction process.

ideas.title: Causal Disambiguation of Experience via Perturbed Neural-Behavioral Manifolds
ideas.description: This research addresses the inverse problem's underdetermination by moving from passive observation to active causal intervention. It builds a joint model of neural activity and behavior, then systematically introduces subtle, ethically-designed experimental perturbations to observe how the neural-behavioral manifold shifts. By correlating these forced manifold displacements with first-person reports of experiential change, the system learns a constrained mapping that can disambiguate which experiential states are consistent with the observed causal structure. This yields a more robust and falsifiable model of the relationship between external signals and internal experience.

ideas.title: Modeling Interoceptive-Affective Pathways in IPVA Referral Networks Using Active Inference
ideas.description: This research synthesizes network analysis of IPVA referral systems with computational active inference models of interoception. It models a survivor's journey through a service network as a process of active inference, where each step (e.g., a referral, a wait, a mismatch) generates interoceptive prediction errors that accumulate as affective distress. By mapping the network's topology and dynamics onto a generative model of interoceptive states, we can simulate the affective cost of different pathway structures. This enables the optimization of referral networks not just for administrative efficiency, but for minimizing the predicted interoceptive-affective toll on survivors, creating systems that are computationally 'empathic' by design.

ideas.title: Computational Micro-Modeling for Classroom IPVA Affect Detection
ideas.description: This research develops a brief, interactive digital protocol for teachers that integrates computational models of conceptual blending. The tool simulates how basic emotional and social cues in a classroom might construct into complex affective states indicative of IPVA, based on a teacher's input of their specific student dynamics. Teachers can then test micro-intervention responses within the simulation to see predicted emotional trajectories, building their confidence and strategic understanding before real-world application. This moves intervention training from static protocols to dynamic, personalized affect modeling.

ideas.title: Applying Metabolic Currency Models to Emotional Valuation in Mental Economy
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from Systems Ecology and Bioenergetics, where energy flows are quantified with a standardized currency to create budgets and models of trade-offs. The analogous solution involves mapping how energy is captured, transformed, allocated, and dissipated within an ecosystem. Adapted to the original idea of emotion's role in mental economy, the research would develop methods to quantify emotional experiences as a form of 'currency' (e.g., measuring psychophysiological expenditure or behavioral opportunity costs). The study would then investigate how this emotional currency is acquired, budgeted across different mental and behavioral needs, invested for long-term adaptive gains, and spent during regulation, thereby revealing the core trade-offs, allocation principles, and efficiency constraints within the economy of mind and behavior.

ideas.title: Co-Designing and Simulating Emotional Models for IPVA Training via Computational Blending
ideas.description: This research fuses human-centered co-design with computational models of conceptual blending to develop next-generation Intimate Partner Violence and Abuse (IPVA) training. Youth and professionals will co-create core scenarios and emotional concepts, which are then formalized into a computational blending model. This model can simulate how different individuals might emotionally construct and blend concepts related to power, control, and relationships, allowing for the dynamic generation of tailored training vignettes. The result is an adaptive training system that tests and refines a learner's understanding by responding in real-time to their unique emotional and conceptual frameworks.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Folding Simulation Methods to Model Conceptual Blending in Emotion Construction
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution methods from computational protein folding to the problem of simulating emotion construction via conceptual blending. In biochemistry, molecular dynamics and Monte Carlo simulations successfully model how simple linear sequences (amino acids) fold into complex, functional 3D structures (proteins) by navigating a high-dimensional energy landscape. This analogous solution is adapted by treating basic affective and appraisal processes as the fundamental elements. A computational model is built where these elements interact within a defined 'psychodynamic force field'—a set of rules governing compatibility, tension, and integration. Simulation methods (e.g., Monte Carlo sampling, agent-based exploration) then computationally drive the blending process, allowing the system to explore the vast space of possible emotional blends. The simulation identifies stable, low-'energy' configurations, which represent coherent, constructed emotional experiences emerging from the dynamic interaction of basic processes, providing a novel computational lens on emotion genesis.

ideas.title: Inverse Psychophysics: A Bayesian Causal Model for Inferring Subjective Experience from Behavioral Traces
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to a classic inverse problem from geophysics—seismic tomography—to the challenge of inferring emotional experience from behavior. In seismic tomography, the internal structure of the Earth is inferred from surface wave measurements using forward models and Bayesian inversion. Here, we propose to construct a 'psychophysical tomography' framework. We will develop a generative causal model that defines the forward process: how a latent variable representing the content and intensity of a subjective emotional experience produces observable behavioral traces across multiple channels. This model will incorporate individual differences as prior distributions. Using high-density behavioral data, we will then apply Bayesian inversion techniques to solve for the posterior distribution over possible experiential states that best explain the observed behavior, thereby directly quantifying the inference's uncertainty and moving from mere correlation to a model-based causal inference of private experience.

ideas.title: Implementing Homeostatic Emotion Models in AI via Cybernetic Control Theory
ideas.description: Scale up the construction framework to implement emotion-like processes in artificial intelligence systems by drawing an analogy from systems physiology and cybernetics. The analogous problem—understanding how biological emotions function as regulatory systems—was solved by modeling emotions as homeostatic control mechanisms that maintain internal equilibrium and guide adaptive behavior. Adapting this solution, the research proposes to implement scalable 'homeostatic emotion models' in AI agents using control-theoretic frameworks. These models would function as dynamic, high-level regulators that continuously monitor the agent's internal state and environmental interactions against predefined viability set-points. By processing prediction errors, these emotion-like controllers would adaptively adjust core cognitive parameters—such as attention allocation, learning rates, and decision-making biases—to maintain system equilibrium and optimize for long-term, survival-aligned objectives within complex, open-ended environments.

ideas.title: Computational Phenotyping of Psychopathology via Predictive Interoceptive-Conceptual Mismatch
ideas.description: This research program empirically tests the psychological construction framework by using it to derive and validate computational phenotypes for psychopathology. It develops tasks that independently quantify interoceptive prediction error and conceptualization efficiency, then tests their interaction in generating symptoms specific to disorders like anxiety, depression, and somatic symptom disorders. The core innovation is using model comparison (e.g., constructionist vs. basic emotion or appraisal models) to explain symptom profiles, moving beyond descriptive diagnosis to identify dysfunctional construction mechanisms as treatment targets.

ideas.title: Modeling the Interoceptive Dynamics of Non-Equal Power Relational Safety in IPVA
ideas.description: This research synthesizes active inference models of interoceptive prediction with critical analysis of relational power dynamics in intimate partner violence and abuse (IPVA) contexts. It develops computational simulations to test how different relational templates (e.g., compassionate authority, distributed expertise) are encoded as interoceptive predictions that foster a sense of safety and support, even in the absence of perfect equality. The work moves beyond theoretical debate to provide a formal, mechanistic account of how the brain's predictive processing of bodily states might adapt to and be shaped by various effective support dynamics, offering new metrics for intervention design.

ideas.title: Assessing the Impact of Trauma-Informed Training on Professional Recognition of Non-Descriptive Emotional Distress in Youth
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that effective trauma-informed responses depend on a youth's ability to articulate rich, descriptive accounts of their emotional experience. It posits that emotional distress, particularly in the context of interpersonal violence, may often be non-descriptive or content-poor. The study will evaluate the effectiveness of trauma-informed training by specifically measuring whether it improves school professionals' ability to accurately recognize, validate, and respond to these subtler, less articulate presentations of distress, rather than waiting for or prompting detailed disclosures. This tests a fundamental synergy: whether training enhances sensitivity to the phenomenological reality of trauma (non-rich emotion) to improve practical response quality.

ideas.title: Mapping the Emotional Economy of IPVA Referral Pathways in Schools
ideas.description: This research investigates how the emotional experiences of adolescents disclosing intimate partner violence and the school staff managing referrals function within a 'mental economy' of cognitive and affective resources. It maps existing referral pathways while simultaneously analyzing the emotional transactions—such as the cost of shame, the investment of trust, or the debt of burnout—that drive or hinder movement through the system. The goal is to develop and test optimized, emotion-aware referral protocols that minimize emotional 'inflation' (e.g., anxiety, re-traumatization) and maximize efficient 'circulation' toward support, fundamentally reconceptualizing pathway design from a purely procedural to a psycho-affective architecture.

ideas.title: Phenomenological Simulation Debriefing for IPVA Response Training
ideas.description: This research adapts high-fidelity medical simulation to train school staff on intimate partner violence and abuse (IPVA) responses, but crucially integrates structured phenomenological interview techniques into the post-simulation debrief. Instead of a standard procedural review, facilitators use phenomenological methods to guide participants in describing their in-the-moment perceptions, judgments, and emotional landscapes during the simulated crisis. This deep, qualitative excavation of the lived experience of the responder transforms the simulation from a skills drill into a rich data-generating event, uncovering hidden cognitive biases, emotional barriers, and tacit knowledge. The collected phenomenological data can then be analyzed to refine training scenarios, develop more empathetic and effective response protocols, and create a framework for 'emotional preparedness' in school staff.

ideas.title: Formally Grounded Homeostatic Drives for Robust and Interpretable Agent Adaptation
ideas.description: This research designs artificial emotion-like processes not as scaled-up black-box simulations, but as explicit, modular mechanisms for dynamic resource and goal management. It implements a core set of quantifiable homeostatic drives and defines emotional states as interpretable, vector-valued readouts of their satisfaction levels. These states are then formally mapped to tunable parameters of the agent's cognitive architecture, such as shifting exploration-exploitation balance or reallocating perceptual bandwidth, creating a transparent, causal link between internal state and adaptive behavior that can be rigorously tested for robustness and utility.

ideas.title: Emotional Resource Mapping for Classroom IPVA Micro-Interventions
ideas.description: This research develops and tests micro-interventions for teachers that are grounded in a model of emotional mental economy. It posits that exposure to IPVA disrupts a student's emotional resource balance (e.g., depleting safety, over-investing in hypervigilance). The protocols train teachers to quickly identify these specific emotional economic disruptions—such as transactional withdrawal or emotional 'inflation' of minor conflicts—and apply brief, classroom-specific actions to recalibrate the student's emotional expenditures and returns. This moves beyond generic response checklists to a dynamic, economy-based strategy for initial containment and support within the educational setting.

ideas.title: National AI Companion Framework for Adolescent Mental Health and IPVA Prevention
ideas.description: This research fuses the goal of a national school-based policy for Interpersonal Violence and Abuse (IPVA) response with the technical framework for modeling emotion in artificial agents. The result is a proposal to design, pilot, and advocate for a standardized national policy that deploys ethically-governed, emotion-modeling AI companions as a primary preventative layer in secondary schools. These agents would be integrated into school digital systems to provide 24/7, non-judgmental interaction, using their emotional modeling to identify subtle shifts in student affect, language, and behavior that may signal risk of IPVA victimization, perpetration, or associated mental health crises. The policy framework would mandate strict privacy safeguards, human-in-the-loop escalation protocols to trained counselors, and continuous evaluation to create a scalable, early-intervention ecosystem that neither training alone nor standalone AI tools could achieve.

ideas.title: Emotion-Modeling AI Agents as Simulated Trainees to Evaluate and Optimize Trauma-Informed Training
ideas.description: This research develops a population of artificial agents endowed with computational models of emotion and stress responses, specifically calibrated to simulate adolescents experiencing intimate partner violence and abuse (IPVA). These agents are then deployed in immersive training simulations as standardized 'trainees' for school professionals. The core investigation is to use the agents' internal emotional states and behavioral outputs as precise, objective metrics to evaluate which components of trauma-informed training most effectively improve the quality of professional interactions. This enables the controlled, iterative optimization of training protocols by testing them against a diverse, scalable, and ethically unconstrained simulated population before deployment in real-world settings.

ideas.title: Adaptive Response Pedagogy for IPVA: Training Cognitive Flexibility and Situational Judgment in School Staff
ideas.description: This system moves beyond high-fidelity procedural simulation to train school staff in the adaptive thinking required for real-world IPVA response. It employs a series of progressively challenging, branching vignettes that introduce ambiguity, emotional intensity, and conflicting duties to stress-test staff judgment under pressure. Each scenario is followed by facilitated 'deconstruction' sessions focusing on the cues that informed decisions and the range of possible valid actions, building a mental toolkit for non-protocolized situations. The approach integrates stress inoculation techniques to improve performance in authentic, messy contexts where textbook procedures often break down.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Theory to Test the Universality of Trauma-Informed IPVA Models
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a standard trauma-informed care model is universally effective for all forms of intimate partner violence and abuse (IPVA). Drawing an analogy from ecology and evolutionary biology, where the universal application of the competitive exclusion principle was refined by niche theory, this research adapts that solution. It posits that different forms of IPVA (such as coercive control, situational couple violence, or separation-instigated violence) constitute distinct 'ecological niches' with unique characteristics. The analogous solution involved mapping niches and testing the fitness of generalist versus specialist species. Adapted here, the research would involve mapping the defining features of IPVA subtypes and then conducting comparative outcome studies to test whether the generalist 'trauma-informed' model or more specialized, form-specific intervention models lead to better outcomes for survivor safety, mental health, and autonomy. This directly tests the universality assumption by seeking evidence for niche-adapted approaches.

ideas.title: Validate EI's incremental predictive validity for social outcomes

ideas.title: Survivor-Defined Safety: Empirically Testing Relational Dynamics in Support Networks for Functional Outcomes in IPVA Contexts
ideas.description: This research shifts the focus from challenging 'equal power as an ideal' to empirically investigating which observable relational dynamics in a survivor's support network (e.g., family, friends, advocates) most effectively contribute to survivor-defined safety and autonomy. It employs a longitudinal, mixed-methods design to track how specific interaction patterns—such as reciprocal exchange, negotiated dependency, and collective agency—functionally correlate with tangible outcomes like successful safety plan execution, reduced re-victimization, and increased well-being. The study is explicitly grounded in the principle that equality remains a critical benchmark for the intimate partner relationship itself, while examining how a diversity of supportive relational forms outside the abusive dynamic can be operationalized and evaluated based on their concrete utility to survivors.

ideas.title: Beyond Equal Power: Applying Ecosystem Stability Models to IPVA Support Dynamics
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that equal power is the sole ideal relational dynamic for fostering safety and effective support in Intimate Partner Violence and Abuse (IPVA) contexts. Drawing an analogy from ecology and systems biology, it parallels the problem of viewing a single 'climax community' as the only stable ecosystem state. The analogous field solved this by recognizing 'alternative stable states' maintained by specific keystone species and functional redundancies. Adapting this solution, the research will explore if other relational dynamics—conceptualized as alternative stable states—can also provide safety and support in IPVA scenarios. It will involve qualitatively mapping these alternative configurations (e.g., consciously negotiated asymmetries, fluid power-sharing models) and identifying the core 'keystone' behaviors, communication patterns, or external supports that make them resilient and effective, thereby expanding the theoretical and practical toolkit for IPVA intervention and support.

ideas.title: A Control-Theoretic Framework for Psychopathology: Modeling Dysfunctions as Faults in Predictive Regulation
ideas.description: This research seed proposes applying analogical reasoning from control systems engineering to the original idea of linking predictive processing to psychopathology. In engineering, complex dynamical systems are managed using control theory, where failures are diagnosed as specific faults in sensors, actuators, or feedback loops, and solutions involve designing precise compensators. By adapting this solution, we can model psychiatric disorders not just as dysfunctions in interoceptive prediction or concept use, but as quantifiable faults within a formal predictive control system. This approach allows for the creation of computational state-space models for different disorders, enabling the simulation of dysfunction mechanisms and the principled design of interventions (therapeutic, pharmacological, or technological) aimed at recalibrating the faulty components to restore healthy regulatory function.
