ideas.title: Empowering Youth as 'First Responders' in Mental Health: A Community Ecology Model
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from community ecology, where building intrinsic ecosystem resilience has proven more sustainable than relying solely on external management. The analogous solution involves shifting from top-down professional intervention to bottom-up empowerment of the system's components. Adapted to youth mental health, the research challenges the assumption that professionals must be the primary frontline responders. Instead, it prioritizes interventions that directly empower youth, equipping them to be agents of mental health promotion and early support within their own social networks. By fostering peer-based knowledge, skills, and supportive roles, the aim is to create more resilient youth communities that can prevent and mitigate crises, thereby fundamentally altering the demand structure for professional crisis response.

ideas.title: Youth-led exploration of relational dynamics beyond equal power for IPVA safety and support
ideas.description: This research inverts the standard approach by positioning youth, not professionals, as the primary investigators of relational dynamics that can foster safety and support in contexts of intimate partner violence and abuse (IPVA). It moves beyond the singular professional ideal of 'equal power' by empowering youth to identify, articulate, and model alternative dynamics—such as fluid reciprocity, protective asymmetry, or peer solidarity—that they find effective within their own social ecologies. The core methodology involves participatory action research where youth co-design and evaluate peer-led interventions, thereby generating a youth-derived taxonomy of supportive relational postures. This directly challenges adult-centric, professionally disseminated models and creates a new evidence base for empowerment strategies rooted in the authentic social logic of young people.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Systems Theory for IPVA Training: A Tiered, Context-Sensitive Protocol
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Ecosystem Ecology and Conservation Biology, this idea adapts the field's solution of using multi-tiered, context-sensitive intervention frameworks. Conservation actions are tailored to specific levels (species, habitat, landscape) and local conditions. Adapted to the research context, the project would develop and test a trauma-informed IPVA training program not as a monolithic course, but as a suite of modular, role-specific protocols. These modules would be customized for different school professional groups (teachers, coaches, counselors, administrators) based on their position in the school's social 'ecosystem' and their nexus with external referral pathways. The training would teach staff to navigate power dynamics and referral processes specific to their professional context, potentially increasing efficacy and appropriate intervention.

ideas.title: Classroom Triage Protocols: A Point-of-Care Diagnostic Model for IPVA
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from emergency medicine's point-of-care diagnostics to the problem of identifying and initially responding to Intimate Partner Violence and Abuse (IPVA) among students. The analogous field uses simple, standardized tools at the frontline to quickly identify critical conditions and trigger an appropriate initial response and referral pathway. The adapted idea is to develop a 'Classroom Triage Protocol'—a micro-intervention consisting of a brief, classroom-specific observational checklist and scripted conversational prompts. This tool would empower teachers to perform a rapid, low-stakes assessment of potential IPVA indicators. It would then provide a clear, immediate decision matrix for a supportive first response (e.g., expressing concern, validating the student) and for determining the urgency of referral to the school's dedicated safeguarding officer, effectively scaling the response from the individual classroom level to the institutional support system.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Network Robustness Metrics to Optimize IPVA Referral Pathways
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from ecological network analysis to the problem of optimizing IPVA (Intimate Partner Violence and Abuse) referral systems. In ecology, network science is used to model interactions like food webs and plant-pollinator networks, with a focus on metrics like modularity and centrality to identify keystone species and simulate network robustness to disruptions. By directly analogizing IPVA service agencies to species and referral pathways to ecological interactions, we can apply similar robustness analysis. This involves modeling the referral network and simulating cascading failures from the removal of key nodes or edges, thereby identifying critical vulnerabilities and 'keystone' support services. The adapted research aims to use these simulations to propose concrete, evidence-based interventions that increase the redundancy and resilience of referral pathways, ensuring the support system remains functional for survivors despite internal or external shocks.

ideas.title: A National IPVA Response Framework: Adapting the 'Minimum Viable Product' Model from Software Development
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from software engineering's approach to scaling solutions. The analogous problem is scaling a software application from a local success to a national platform, which is solved using a Minimum Viable Product (MVP) framework that balances core standardization with local customization. Adapted to the context of Intimate Partner Violence and Adolescence (IPVA) response in schools, the research would design and advocate for a national policy framework modeled on this approach. The core output would be a 'Minimum Viable Framework'—a standardized, mandatory set of essential protocols for identification, immediate response, and reporting that establishes a national baseline of care and safety. Crucially, the framework's design would mandate this core while providing a structured mechanism for states, districts, and individual schools to integrate additional, tailored support services, training programs, and community partnerships, thus enabling scalable national implementation that respects local needs and resources.

ideas.title: Applying Participatory Action Research from Community Ecology to Co-Design IPVA Training
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Participatory Action Research (PAR) methodology, commonly used in community ecology and environmental management, to the co-design of IPVA training programs. In the analogous field, PAR successfully engages local communities as co-researchers to ensure conservation solutions are effective and sustainable. By transferring this approach, we propose forming a collaborative team of youth (with lived or adjacent experience) and professionals (e.g., social workers, educators). This team would jointly define the core challenges in IPVA education, co-create prototype training materials, conduct pilot sessions, and use cyclical feedback to refine the program. This ensures the final training is not just informed by, but actively shaped by, the end-users, leading to greater relevance, uptake, and impact.

ideas.title: Adaptive Implementation System for School-Based IPVA Response
ideas.description: This research designs and advocates for a national 'Adaptive Implementation System' rather than a rigid policy. The system provides a core architecture of principles and a repository of evidence-based, modular interventions for addressing intimate partner violence among adolescents (IPVA) in schools. It establishes a structured process and support network for districts to select, contextually adapt, and implement these modules, with a built-in mechanism to collect fidelity and outcome data. This data then feeds back into a national learning collaborative to continuously refine the modules and implementation guidance, creating a responsive, evidence-generating ecosystem that balances national coordination with local autonomy and effectiveness.

ideas.title: An Implementation Support System for Embedding IPVA-Informed Practices in School Climates
ideas.description: This research develops and tests a multi-component Implementation Support System (ISS) designed to durably embed IPVA-informed knowledge and practices within schools. The ISS combines initial trauma-informed training with ongoing coaching for professionals, the formation of school-based response teams, and continuous data collection on fidelity and student well-being indicators. The system is designed to be adaptive, using feedback loops to refine local referral pathways and support structures, thereby moving beyond one-off training to create sustained organizational change and improve systemic responses to IPVA.

ideas.title: Applying Network Flow Optimization to Map and Improve IPVA Referral Pathways in Schools
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from transportation engineering, this research adapts network flow optimization techniques to the problem of improving school-based referral systems for youth experiencing intimate partner violence (IPVA). In transportation, engineers model networks to find bottlenecks and optimize the flow of goods; here, we model the referral system as a network where 'nodes' are school staff and external support services, and 'edges' are the referral pathways between them, characterized by metrics like time-to-connect and success rate. The analogous solution involves using network analysis and optimization algorithms (e.g., to find minimum-cost or maximum-flow paths) to diagnose systemic inefficiencies and vulnerabilities. The adapted research idea involves mapping the current IPVA referral network within a school district, applying these computational models to identify critical failure points and suboptimal routes, and then generating data-driven proposals for restructuring pathways, adding direct connections, or increasing capacity through staff training to create a more efficient, resilient, and survivor-centered referral system.

ideas.title: Adapt Crew Resource Management (CRM) simulation from aviation for school IPVA response training
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Crew Resource Management (CRM) simulation training paradigm from aviation safety to the context of school staff responses to Intimate Partner Violence Affecting children (IPVA). In aviation, CRM uses high-fidelity simulators to immerse crews in rare, high-consequence emergency scenarios, drilling standardized communication, clear role assignment, and collective decision-making. Adapted for schools, this involves developing immersive, multi-staff simulation scenarios (e.g., a student disclosure, a parent incident on campus) within a simulated school environment. The training would focus not just on individual procedural knowledge, but on practicing the team-based coordination, stress-tested communication, and protocol-driven decision-making required for an effective, systemic IPVA response, thereby increasing preparedness and reducing error in real incidents.

ideas.title: Peer Support Cascade Training: Adapting Epidemiological Contact Tracing Models for IPVA Intervention
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original 'inverse problem' idea by drawing an analogy from epidemiology and public health. The analogous problem is scaling health interventions within resistant or hard-to-reach populations (e.g., for HIV prevention). The analogous solution is the use of peer-driven intervention or contact tracing models, where trained individuals recruit and train their own social contacts, creating a cascade effect that leverages trust networks for exponential scaling. Adapted to the context of youth peer support for IPVA, the research idea investigates designing and testing a 'cascade training' protocol. Initially trained youth peer supporters would not only provide support but also actively identify and recruit candidates from their social circles for the next wave of training. This creates a self-propagating system that uses the organic structure of youth social networks to scale the intervention, potentially increasing its penetration, cultural relevance, and sustainability compared to traditional, professionally-managed peer supporter programs. The study would examine the efficacy, fidelity, and dynamics of this peer-driven scaling model for IPVA prevention and support.

ideas.title: A Participatory System Dynamics and Network Optimization Framework for IPVA Referral Pathways
ideas.description: This research integrates participatory system dynamics modeling with constrained network optimization to move beyond descriptive mapping. Survivors and frontline providers first co-create causal loop diagrams to identify and weight hidden social and institutional barriers within referral networks. These dynamic constraints are then formalized within a multi-objective optimization model that redesigns network connections, balancing traditional efficiency metrics with survivor-centric outcomes like safety, trust, and choice. The result is a prescriptive, ethically-grounded framework for pathway redesign that is robust to real-world systemic failures.

ideas.title: Youth-Led Participatory Action Networks for IPVA Prevention
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a shift from training youth as passive peer supporters to establishing youth-led participatory action networks for IPVA prevention. It integrates the empowerment focus of Idea A with the peer-support structure of Idea B by positioning youth as co-researchers and intervention architects. These networks would design, deliver, and iteratively adapt peer-led initiatives, training protocols, and awareness campaigns, thereby embedding sustainable prevention capacity directly within youth communities. The synergy transforms a service-delivery model into a generative system of youth-driven social change.

ideas.title: Dual-Pathway Empowerment: Coupling Youth Agency with Mandatory Structural Engagement
ideas.description: This approach rejects a binary choice between youth empowerment and professional training, instead designing interventions that bind the two in a mandatory feedback loop. A youth cohort is equipped with specific advocacy skills and project resources, while the relevant adult professionals and gatekeepers in their ecosystem are concurrently trained with a focus on responsiveness and are held accountable to a formal mechanism for integrating youth input. Research measures both the development of youth agency and the measurable shifts in institutional policies, resource allocation, and professional practices that result from this coupled design, ensuring empowerment is not an isolated event but a system-wide reconfiguration.

ideas.title: Embedded First Response: School-Wide Infrastructure for Classroom IPVA Protocols
ideas.description: This research develops and tests a coordinated school-wide system where classroom-specific IPVA identification protocols for teachers are directly linked to an immediate institutional response. The system design includes brief, scripted teacher actions paired with a streamlined, confidential method to instantly alert a pre-designated on-site support team (e.g., counselor, nurse, administrator). The research will measure the efficacy of this embedded infrastructure in reducing teacher burden and stress, improving response consistency, and ensuring timely, specialist-led support for students, comparing outcomes to teacher-only intervention models.

ideas.title: National Youth-Led IPVA Peer Support Corps: A Policy-Driven Model for Systemic School Safety
ideas.description: This research designs and advocates for a national policy framework that mandates and resources the establishment of a Youth-Led IPVA Peer Support Corps within secondary schools. The policy standardizes core training, ethical safeguards, and supervision protocols for student peer supporters, ensuring fidelity and safety while embedding their role into official school response pathways. This creates a dual-impact system: it provides scalable, accessible first-line support for peers experiencing IPVA, and it systematically cultivates youth leadership and advocacy skills, building a national cohort of informed change agents. The model shifts the paradigm from adult-centric intervention to a co-produced, sustainable safety ecosystem governed by policy but driven by youth.
